Ayahs

سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (١)
Whatever there is in the heavens and [whatever there is on] the earth glorifies Allah and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (1)
لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٢)
To Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth: He gives life and brings death, and He has power over all things. (2)
هُوَ ٱلْأَوَّلُ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرُ وَٱلظَّـٰهِرُ وَٱلْبَاطِنُ ۖ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (٣)
He is the First and the Last, the Manifest and the Hidden, and He has knowledge of all things. (3)

Content of Surah al-Hadid

Given that this Surah is among those revealed in Medina—and some have even claimed a consensus on its "Medinan" origin—it naturally possesses the characteristics of Medinan Surahs. In addition to strengthening ideological foundations, it addresses numerous practical instructions, especially in social and governmental spheres, examples of which we will see in verses 10, 11, and 25, God willing.

The content of this Surah can be divided into seven sections:

  1. The opening verses of the Surah contain a comprehensive and fascinating discussion regarding Monotheism (Tawhid) and the Attributes of Allah. Approximately twenty Divine Attributes are reflected therein, the understanding of which places a human being at a high level of Divine gnosis.

  2. Another section speaks of the greatness of the Quran, this Divine Light that shone within the darkness of polytheism.

  3. The third section discusses the state of the believers and the hypocrites on the Day of Resurrection, where the first group opens their way toward Paradise in the light of their faith, while the second group remains in the darkness of polytheism and disbelief. Thus, the three principles of Islam—Monotheism, Prophethood, and the Resurrection—are well-reflected in this Surah.

  4. Another section reflects the call to faith, the departure from polytheism, and the fate of several former disbelieving nations.

  5. A significant portion of this Surah concerns spending (Infaq) in the way of Allah, especially for strengthening the foundations of Jihad in the cause of Allah, and the worthlessness of worldly wealth.

  6. In a brief but eloquent and reasoned section, social justice is discussed, which is one of the primary goals of the Prophets.

  7. Finally, in another section, the issue of monasticism and social isolation is condemned, and the distinct path of Islam is clearly defined from it.

Of course, other points are mentioned throughout these discussions as appropriate, ultimately forming an awakening and guiding collection.

Furthermore, the naming of this Surah as "al-Hadid" (Iron) is due to the expression mentioned in verse 25 of the Surah.

The Virtue of Reciting Surah al-Hadid

In Islamic narrations, noteworthy points have been mentioned regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah—provided, of course, that the recitation is accompanied by reflection, and the reflection is accompanied by action.

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Whoever recites Surah al-Hadid will be recorded among those who believed in Allah and His Messenger." [^1]

In another Hadith narrated from the same Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is mentioned that before sleeping, He would recite the "Musabbihat" (the Musabbihat are the Surahs that begin with Sabbaha lillah or Yusabbihu lillah, and they are five: Hadid, Hashr, Saff, Jumu'ah, and Taghabun), and He said: "Indeed, among them is a verse that is better than a thousand verses!" [^2]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, beginning of Surah al-Hadid. [^2]: Ibid, plus al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 170.

Of course, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not specify this verse, but some commentators have speculated that it refers to the last verse of Surah al-Hashr, although there is no clear evidence for this meaning [^1]. In another Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever recites all the Musabbihat before he sleeps shall not die until he perceives the Qa'im (the Riser) عليه السلام, and if he dies [before then], he will be in the neighborhood of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله."

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, beginning of Surah al-Hadid.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ هُوَ الْعَزِیزُ الْحَكِیمُ (1) لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ یُحْیِي وَ یُمِیتُ وَ هُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِیرٌ (2) هُوَ الْأَوَّلُ وَ الْآخِرُ وَ الظَّاهِرُ وَ الْباطِنُ وَ هُوَ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِیمٌ (3)

Commentary

Verses for Deep Thinkers!

We mentioned that this Surah begins with a section on Monotheism that encompasses about "twenty descriptions" of the Divine Attributes; attributes whose recognition raises the level of human knowledge and acquaints one with His Holy Essence. Each of these attributes points to an aspect of His Attributes of Majesty (Jalal) and Beauty (Jamal), and the more thinkers reflect upon them, the more new truths they discover.

As we read in a Hadith from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام: "When he was asked about Monotheism, he replied: 'Indeed, Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, knew that in the end of times there would be people who delve deeply [into matters]; therefore, Allah the Exalted revealed: Say, He is Allah, the One and the verses of Surah al-Hadid up to His saying: and He is Knowing of that within the breasts. So whoever seeks beyond that has perished.'" [^1] From this Hadith, it is understood that these verses provide the maximum possible knowledge to those thirsty for it.

In any case, the first verse of this Surah begins with the glorification and sanctification of Allah, stating: "Whatever is in the heavens and the earth constantly glorifies Allah, and He is the All-Mighty, the All-Wise" (سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ هُوَ الْعَزِیزُ الْحَكِیمُ).

The previous Surah ended with a command to glorify, and this Surah begins with Divine glorification. It is noteworthy that in the "Musabbihat" Surahs—which begin with Divine glorification—in three instances, the glorification appears in the past tense (Sabbaha) (Hadid, Hashr, and Saff), and in two instances, it appears in the present/future tense (Yusabbihu) (Jumu'ah and Taghabun).

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 231.

This difference in expression perhaps points to the fact that in the past, future, and always, the beings of this world have glorified and continue to glorify His Holy Essence.

The reality of glorification (Tasbih) consists of negating every kind of defect and flaw [^1]. The testimony of all beings in the world to the purity of His Holy Essence from any defect or flaw is either because such order, calculation, wisdom, and wonders are employed in the system of them all that they collectively speak of the Truth with the "tongue of their state" (zaban-e hal), reciting His glorification and praise, crying out that our Creator's power is infinite and His wisdom is endless.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, the phrase "and He is the All-Mighty, the All-Wise" (وَ هُوَ الْعَزِیزُ الْحَكِیمُ) appears. Alternatively, it may be that all particles of the world possess a kind of perception and consciousness such that they glorify and praise Allah in their own realm, even though we are unaware of it due to our limited information.

For a more detailed explanation regarding the general praise and glorification of the beings of the world, please study the commentary on verse 44 of Surah al-Isra (Vol. 12, pp. 133 to 136).

It is also necessary to remind that "Ma" (Whatever) in the phrase "Whatever is in the heavens" (سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ) has a broad and expansive meaning that includes all beings of the world, whether they are possessors of intellect, animate, or inanimate [^2].

[^1]: Tasbih originally comes from the root Sabh (on the pattern of Mash), meaning rapid movement in water or air. Tasbih is also rapid movement in the path of worshipping the Lord (Raghib in al-Mufradat). [^2]: Although Tasbih can be transitive without a preposition, for example, it is said Sabbahuhu, here it is mentioned with Lam, which may be for emphasis.

After mentioning two descriptions of the Essence of Allah, namely "Might" and "Wisdom," the verse proceeds to His "Ownership, Governance, and Disposal in the universe," which are requirements of Power and Wisdom, adding: "To Him belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth" (لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ).

"He gives life and causes death" (یُحْيِي وَ یُمِیتُ).

"And He is over all things competent" (وَ هُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِیرٌ).

Allah's ownership of the universe is not a conventional or legislative ownership, but rather a real and ontological ownership. That is, He encompasses everything, and the entire world is in the grasp of His power and under His will and command. Therefore, this is followed by the mention of giving life, causing death, and the ability over all things.

Thus, up to this point, six attributes of His descriptions have been stated in these two verses.

The difference between "Might" (Izzat) and "Power" (Qudrat) is that Might focuses more on breaking the defender, while Power focuses on creating the means. Therefore, they are considered two distinct descriptions, even though they share the root of capability (reflect on this).

The issue of giving life and causing death (Ihya and Imatah) is raised in many verses of the Quran. In reality, these two subjects are among those whose complex secrets are clear to no one. No one truly knows the reality of life, nor does anyone know the reality of death; rather, what we know of these two are their effects. It is strange that the closest thing to us is our life, yet its reality and secrets are more hidden than anything else! It is noteworthy that the phrase "He gives life and causes death" (Yuhyi wa Yumit) is in the present/future tense, indicating the continuity of the phenomenon of life and death throughout all times. The absolute nature of these two includes not only the life and death of humans in this world but every kind of life and death, from the realm of angels to other living beings, animals, and plants. It encompasses not only the life of this world but also the life of the Barzakh and the Resurrection. Yes, life and death in all their forms are in the hand of His power.

Then, expressing five other attributes, He says: "He is the First and the Last, the Manifest and the Hidden, and He is, of all things, Knowing" (هُوَ الْأَوَّلُ وَ الْآخِرُ وَ الظَّاهِرُ وَ الْباطِنُ وَ هُوَ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِیمٌ).

Describing Him as the First and the Last is a subtle expression of His eternity and infinity. For we know He is an infinite Being and the Necessary Existent (Wajib al-Wujud), meaning His existence is from within His Essence, not from without, such that it would end or have a beginning. Therefore, He has been from eternity and will be until forever.

He is the origin and beginning of the universe, and He is the One who will remain after the annihilation of the world.

Thus, the expression "First and Last" never encompasses a specific time and does not refer to a certain duration.

Describing Him as the Manifest and the Hidden is another expression of His existential encompassment of everything. He is more manifest than anything because His signs have filled everywhere, and He is more hidden than anything because His Essence is not clear to anyone.

Some commentators have expressed it this way: "The First without a beginning, the Last without an end, the Manifest without proximity, and the Hidden without concealment."

Others have another subtle expression: "He is the First in His goodness, the Last in His forgiveness, the Manifest in His grace and success when you obey Him, and the Hidden in His veiling when you disobey Him."

In short, He encompasses everything; He is the beginning, the end, the manifest, and the hidden of the universe.

Some commentators have interpreted "Manifest" (Zahir) here to mean "Dominant" (from Zuhur meaning victory), and in some sermons of Nahj al-Balagha, there is evidence for this meaning where, regarding the creation of the earth, it says: "He is the Manifest over it by His authority and greatness, and He is the Hidden to it by His knowledge and awareness." [^1] There is no obstacle to combining both interpretations.

In any case, one of the results of these matters is what comes at the end of the verse: "And He is, of all things, Knowing" (وَ هُوَ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِیمٌ), because one who has been from the beginning and remains until the end, and is in the manifest and hidden of the world, such a person is certainly aware of everything.

Key Point: The Coincidence of Opposites in Allah's Attributes

There are many attributes that are irreconcilable in us humans and other beings, appearing as contradictory traits. For example, if I am the first person in a crowd, I certainly will not be the last. If I am manifest, I am not hidden, and if I am hidden, I will not be manifest. All of this is because our existence is limited, and a limited existence cannot be otherwise. However, when it comes to the Attributes of Allah, the descriptions transform and change shape. There, manifest and hidden coincide, as do beginning and end. Given the infinity of His Holy Essence, this is not surprising.

In the Hadiths reaching us from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام in this regard, interesting explanations are seen that help in interpreting these profound verses.

Among them, in a Hadith in Sahih Muslim, it is stated that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "O Allah! You are the First, so there is nothing before You; You are the Last, so there is nothing after You; You are the Manifest, so there is nothing above You; and You are the Hidden, so there is nothing beyond You." [^1]

Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام says: "There is no beginning to His firstness, nor is there an end to His eternity. He is the First who has always been, and the Lasting without a term... He is the Manifest of whom it cannot be asked 'From what?'; and the Hidden of whom it cannot be asked 'In what?'" [^2]

Imam Hasan al-Mujtaba عليه السلام also says in a sermon: "Praise be to Allah for whom there is no known first, nor a finite last... Thus, intellects and their imaginations, thoughts and their reflections, and minds and their perceptions cannot grasp His description, such that they might say 'When?'; nor is He an innovation 'From what?'; nor is He manifest 'Upon what?'; nor hidden 'In what?'" [^1]

How long will you boast of your intellect, O philosopher? This path cannot be traversed by the intellect. The intellect might find the way to His Essence, Only if a straw could reach the bottom of the sea!

Rather, it can be said: The intellect finds no way to His Essence, Even if a straw could reach the bottom of the sea!

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 9, p. 6406. [^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 163. [^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 236.

Ayahs

هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا يَلِجُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا يَخْرُجُ مِنْهَا وَمَا يَنزِلُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَمَا يَعْرُجُ فِيهَا ۖ وَهُوَ مَعَكُمْ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٤)
It is He who created the heavens and the earth in six days; then settled on the Throne. He knows whatever enters the earth and whatever emerges from it and whatever descends from the sky and whatever ascends to it, and He is with you wherever you may be, and Allah watches what you do. (4)
لَّهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ (٥)
To Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and to Allah all matters are returned. (5)
يُولِجُ ٱلَّيْلَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَيُولِجُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ ۚ وَهُوَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (٦)
He makes the night pass into the day and makes the day pass into the night, and He knows best what is in the breasts. (6)

Commentary

He is Always upon the Throne of Power

Following the eleven attributes mentioned in the previous verses regarding the Pure Essence of the Lord, these verses describe further attributes.

In the first verse under discussion, five other attributes of His Beauty and Glory (Jamal and Jalal) are mentioned.

First, it speaks of the matter of Creation and says: "He is the One who created the heavens and the earth in six days" (هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ).

The issue of "creation in six days" is mentioned seven times in the Holy Quran, the first being in verse 54 of Al-A'raf, and the last being this very verse under discussion (verse 4 of Al-Hadid).

As we have stated previously, the meaning of "day" (yawm) in these verses is not an ordinary day, but rather it means a "period" or "aeon," whether this period is short or long, even if it lasts millions of years. This is an expression used in the Arabic language and other languages as well; for example, it is said: "Today is the turn of such-and-all people to rule, and tomorrow is the turn of others," meaning their era or period.

We have provided this meaning with evidence and a detailed explanation in Volume 6 under verse 54 of Surah Al-A'raf. [^1] Of course, there was no obstacle for Allah to create the entire universe in a single moment, but certainly, if it were so, the greatness, power, and knowledge of the Creator would have been less apparent.

[^1]: "Tafsir Nemouneh," Vol. 6, page 200 onwards.

However, when He creates it over billions of years through different periods and various forms according to organized and calculated plans, it contains clearer proofs of His Power and Wisdom.

Furthermore, this gradual process serves as a model for the gradual nature of man's evolutionary journey and the avoidance of haste and precipitation in reaching various goals.

Then, addressing the matter of the governance and administration of the world, it adds: "Then He established Himself upon the Throne" (ثُمَّ اسْتَوى‌ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ).

And He has always held, and continues to hold, the reins of governance and the administration of the world.

Undoubtedly, Allah is not a physical body, nor is the "Throne" (Arsh) a "royal seat"; rather, this expression is a subtle metaphor for the absolute sovereignty of Allah and the influence of His administration over the realm of existence.

"Arsh"—literally meaning something roofed, and sometimes referring to the roof itself—also means high thrones like those of monarchs, but it is also used as a metaphor for power. Just as in Persian we say: "The pillars of his throne collapsed," or in Arabic they say "Fulan thulla arshuhu," which is a metaphor meaning his power vanished. [^1] In any case, contrary to what some uninformed groups imagine—that Allah created the world and left it to itself—He always holds the reins of governance and the administration of the world in the hand of His Power. The dependence of the systems of this world, and indeed every single being, upon His Pure Essence is such that if He were to withdraw His gaze of grace for a single moment and cut off His bounty, "the forms would collapse"!

[^1]: Regarding the reality of the "Arsh," we have provided more explanations in Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 204 (under verse 54 of Al-A'raf), and in Volume 2, page 200 (under verse 255 of Al-Baqarah).

Attention to this reality gives man an insight and vision to see and feel God everywhere, with everything, and within his own soul, and to love Him.

Then, describing another branch of His infinite knowledge, it adds: "He knows what penetrates into the earth and what emerges from it, and what descends from the heaven and what ascends into it" (يَعْلَمُ ما يَلِجُ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ ما يَخْرُجُ مِنْها وَ ما يَنْزِلُ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَ ما يَعْرُجُ فِيها).

Although all these matters are encompassed in the expression "He is All-Knowing of all things" mentioned in the previous verses, the detailed explanation of these meanings draws man's attention more closely to the vastness of Allah's knowledge.

Yes, He is aware of what goes into the earth: of all the raindrops. Of the waves of floods. Of the seeds of plants that are scattered and penetrate the earth with the help of wind or insects. Of the roots of trees as they push deep into the earth in search of water and nutrients. Of the various types of minerals and treasures that were once on the surface of the earth and were then buried in it. Of treasures and hoards. Of the bodies of the dead. Of the various types of insects that build nests in the ground. Yes, He is aware of all of these.

And likewise, of the plants that emerge from the earth. Of the springs that gush from the heart of the soil and rocks. Of the minerals and treasures that become manifest. Of the human beings who have risen from it. Of the volcanoes that flare up from the heart of the earth. Of the insects that emerge from their nests. Of the gases that rise from the earth. And of the gravitational waves that arise from within it; Allah is aware of all these, part by part and atom by atom.

And similarly, what descends from the heaven: From raindrops to the life-giving rays of the sun. From the hosts of angels to the powerful waves of revelation and the Heavenly Books. From cosmic rays to meteors and wandering pebbles that are attracted toward the earth; He is aware of all these in the most minute detail.

And also what ascends to the heaven: whether it be angels, the souls of humans, the deeds of servants, various types of supplications, kinds of birds, vapors, clouds, and other than these; what we know and what we do not know are all clear and manifest before His knowledge.

If we reflect a little on the fact that at every moment millions upon millions of different beings enter the earth, and millions upon millions of beings rise from it, and millions upon millions descend from the heaven or ascend to the heaven—which are beyond count and limit, and none but Allah can enumerate them—then we become acquainted with the vastness of the Lord's knowledge.

Finally, in the fourth and fifth descriptions, it emphasizes a sensitive point, saying: "And He is with you wherever you are" (وَ هُوَ مَعَكُمْ أَيْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ).

"And since it is so, He is All-Seeing of what you do" (وَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ).

How could He not be with us when we are dependent on Him and seek help from Him moment by moment, not only in our existence but in our survival? He is the Spirit of the realm of existence, He is the Soul of the world—rather, He is higher than this and that! From the time we were a particle of dust lying in a corner, and from the moment we were a fetus in the mother's womb, He was with us; and throughout life, and in the world of Barzakh, He is with us everywhere. In such a state, is it possible for Him to be unaware of us?! Truly, this feeling that He is everywhere with us, on one hand, grants man greatness and splendor, and on the other hand, provides certainty and self-confidence, creating courage and bravery in him. On a third hand, it grants a sense of intense responsibility, for He is present, witnessing, and watching everywhere. This is the greatest lesson of education; yes, this belief is the primary root of man's piety, purity, and integrity, as well as the secret of his greatness and nobility.

It is a reality that He is always and everywhere with us, not a metaphor or figurative speech; a reality that is, on one hand, pleasant, delightful, and soul-nurturing, and on the other hand, awe-inspiring and responsibility-creating! Therefore, it is narrated in a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "Indeed, the highest stage of a person's faith is to know that Allah the Almighty is with him wherever he may be." [^1]

In another hadith, we read that Moses (peace be upon him) said: "Where do I find You, O Lord?" The address came: "O Moses, whenever you intend for Me, you have reached Me!" [^2] Fundamentally, this "Ma'iyyah" (God's being with His servants) is so subtle and precise that every thinking and believing person perceives it and becomes aware of its depth according to the extent of his thought and faith.

[^1]: Ad-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 171. [^2]: Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 351.

After the matter of sovereignty and administration, the discourse reaches the matter of His ownership of the entire universe; it says: "To Him belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth" (لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ). And finally, it points to the matter of His being the ultimate destination, adding: "And to Allah all matters are returned" (وَ إِلَى اللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ الْأُمُورُ).

Yes, when He is our Creator, Owner, Ruler, and Administrator, and is with us everywhere, then certainly the return of all of us and all affairs is toward Him. We are travelers toward the station of His love, who have set out with a bundle of hope and strength from the starting point of non-existence, and have come all this way to the realm of existence; we are from Him and to Him we return, for He is the Beginning and the End.

It is noteworthy that in the three preceding verses, this exact description appeared: "To Him belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth." This repetition may be because there, it spoke only of the matter of life and death of living beings, whereas here the scope of the discussion has broadened, speaking of the return of all affairs to Him.

Also, there it was a prelude to stating Allah's power over all things, while here it is a prelude to the return of everything to Him, both of which are necessary consequences of Allah's ownership of the heavens and the earth.

The expression "al-umur" (the matters) in the plural form shows that not only humans but all beings are in motion toward Him—a permanent and unstoppable motion! Therefore, the concept of the verse is not limited to the return of humans to Him in the Hereafter, although the subject of the Resurrection is considered one of its clear instances.

In the last verse under discussion, it points to two other attributes, saying: "He causes the night to enter the day, and causes the day to enter the night" (يُولِجُ اللَّيْلَ فِي النَّهارِ وَ يُولِجُ النَّهارِ فِي اللَّيْلِ). [^1] Yes, He gradually decreases from one and adds to the other, changing the length of night and day throughout the year—the same change that accompanies the four seasons of the year with all the blessings hidden in these seasons for mankind.

This verse has another interpretation as well, which is that at sunrise and sunset, the change in the system of light and darkness never occurs suddenly so as to cause various problems for humans and other living beings; rather, it occurs gradually, moving creatures slowly from the light of day to the darkness of night and from the darkness of night to the light of day, announcing the arrival of night and day some time in advance so that everyone may prepare themselves.

Combining both interpretations within the concept of the verse is also entirely possible.

[^1]: "Yuliju" is derived from "Iylaj," which in turn is from "Wuluj." "Wuluj" means to enter or penetrate, and "Iylaj" means to cause to enter or to make penetrate.

And at the end, it adds: "And He is All-Knowing of what is within the hearts" (وَ هُوَ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ).

Just as the life-giving rays of the sun and the light of day penetrate the depths of the night's darkness and illuminate everywhere, the knowledge of the Lord penetrates all corners of the human heart and soul and illuminates all its secrets.

It is noteworthy that in the previous verses, the talk was of Allah's awareness of our actions (وَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ), and here the talk is of His awareness of our intentions, beliefs, and thoughts (وَ هُوَ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ).

The word "Dhat," as we have pointed out before, does not mean "essence or reality" in the Arabic language; that is a term used by philosophers. Rather, "Dhat" in the language means "possessor of something." Therefore, "Dhat al-Sudur" refers to the intentions and beliefs that have taken possession of human hearts and rule over them (reflect on this).

And how beautiful it is for a person to believe in all these Divine attributes from the depths of his soul and to feel His presence in his actions, intentions, and beliefs. With such a feeling, is it possible to deviate from the path of obedience and servitude and step onto the path of rebellion and ugliness?!

Key Points The Verses of the Greatest Name of Allah

We know that philosophers and theologians have divided the attributes of God into two categories: "Attributes of Essence," which describe His attributes of Beauty and Glory, and "Attributes of Action," which describe the actions that proceed from His Blessed Essence.

In the six verses that appear at the beginning of this Surah—which, according to the hadith, should be called the verses for "deep thinkers" (mut'ammiqin)—twenty attributes from the attributes of Essence and Action are mentioned, ranging from Allah's knowledge, power, wisdom, eternity, and everlastingness, to His creation, administration, ownership, sovereignty, and His encompassment of all beings, as well as His presence everywhere—and all this with expressions that grant them greater depth.

Attention and faith in these attributes, and the effort to kindle even a small flame of them within one's own being, is the best helper and aid for us in the evolutionary journey and the path toward Allah.

In a hadith from Bara' ibn 'Azib, it is narrated that he said: I said to Ali عليه السلام: "O Commander of the Faithful! I ask you by Allah and His Messenger, that you grant me the greatest thing that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله specifically gave to you, and which Gabriel specifically gave to him, and which the All-Merciful sent him with!" He said: "When you wish to call upon Allah by His Greatest Name (Ism al-A'zam), then read from the beginning of Surah Al-Hadid until the end of six verses, up to 'All-Knowing of what is within the hearts' (عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ), and the end of Surah Al-Hashr, meaning four verses. Then raise your hands and say: 'O He who is thus, I ask You by the right of these names that You send blessings upon Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله and that You do for me such and such'—mentioning what you want. By Allah, besides whom there is no other deity, you shall return with your need fulfilled, if Allah wills." [^1]

Regarding the greatness of these verses and the importance of their content, this hadith alone is sufficient; but it must not be forgotten that the Greatest Name of Allah is not merely words; adopting their character (takhalluq) is also necessary.

[^1]: Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 351.

Ayahs

ءَامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَأَنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا جَعَلَكُم مُّسْتَخْلَفِينَ فِيهِ ۖ فَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَأَنفَقُوا۟ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ (٧)
Have faith in Allah and His Apostle, and spend out of that of which He has made you heirs. There is a great reward for those of you who have faith and spend [in Allah’s way]. (7)
وَمَا لَكُمْ لَا تُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ ۙ وَٱلرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ لِتُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِرَبِّكُمْ وَقَدْ أَخَذَ مِيثَـٰقَكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٨)
Why should you not have faith in Allah when the Apostle invites you to have faith in your Lord, and He has certainly made a covenant with you, if you are [genuinely] faithful? (8)
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُنَزِّلُ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِۦٓ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ لِّيُخْرِجَكُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُمْ لَرَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٩)
It is He who sends down manifest signs to His servant that He may bring you out of darkness into light, and indeed Allah is most kind and merciful to you. (9)
وَمَا لَكُمْ أَلَّا تُنفِقُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِلَّهِ مِيرَٰثُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ لَا يَسْتَوِى مِنكُم مَّنْ أَنفَقَ مِن قَبْلِ ٱلْفَتْحِ وَقَـٰتَلَ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَعْظَمُ دَرَجَةً مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنفَقُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدُ وَقَـٰتَلُوا۟ ۚ وَكُلًّا وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (١٠)
Why should you not spend in the way of Allah, when to Allah belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth? Those of you who spent [their means] and fought before the victory are not equal [to others]. They are greater in rank than those who have spent and fought afterwards. Yet Allah has promised the best reward to each and Allah is well aware of what you do. (10)
مَّن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يُقْرِضُ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا فَيُضَـٰعِفَهُۥ لَهُۥ وَلَهُۥٓ أَجْرٌ كَرِيمٌ (١١)
Who is it that will lend Allah a good loan, that He may multiply it for him and [that] there may be a noble reward for him? (11)

Commentary

Faith and Spending—Two Great Assets for Salvation and Happiness

After explaining some of the proofs of Allah's greatness in the universe and His attributes of beauty and majesty—attributes that motivate movement toward Allah—the verses draw a conclusion from them and invite everyone to faith and action.

First, He says: "Believe in Allah and His Messenger" (آمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رَسُولِهِ).

This is a general invitation that includes all human beings; it calls believers to a more perfect and firm faith, and non-believers to the principle of faith itself—an invitation accompanied by proof, the evidence of which was presented in the preceding verses of monotheism.

Then, He invites to one of the important effects of faith, which is "spending in the way of Allah," saying: "And spend out of that in which He has made you successors" (وَ أَنْفِقُوا مِمَّا جَعَلَكُمْ مُسْتَخْلَفِينَ فِيهِ).

This is a call to altruism, sacrifice, and letting go of the blessings one possesses. However, He couples this invitation with a point: do not forget that "in reality, the original Owner is Allah," and these properties and assets are entrusted to you for a few days, just as they were previously in the possession of past nations.

And indeed, it is so; for we read in the previous verses that the true Owner of the entire universe is Allah. Faith in this reality signifies that we are His trustees. How can a trustee ignore the command of the Owner of the trust?! Faith in this truth grants man a spirit of generosity and sacrifice, and opens his hand and heart to spending.

The expression "mustakhlafin" (successors) may refer to man's representation of Allah on earth and its blessings, or to succeeding previous nations, or both.

The expression "mimma" (out of that which...) is a general term that includes not only wealth but all divine assets and blessings. As we have said before, "spending" (infaq) has a broad meaning that is not limited to money; it also includes knowledge, guidance, social status, and other spiritual and material assets.

Then, for further encouragement, He adds: "Those of you who believe and spend will have a great reward" (فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَ أَنْفَقُوا لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ).

Describing the reward as "great" is an allusion to the majesty of Divine favors and blessings, and their eternity, purity, and permanence. Not only in the Hereafter, but a portion of this great reward reaches them in this world as well.

After the command to "faith" and "spending," He addresses each of these two in a manner that serves as an argument and proof. First, in the form of a rebuking interrogation, He asks the reason for not accepting the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله invitation regarding faith in Allah, saying: "What is the matter with you that you do not believe in Allah, while the Messenger invites you to believe in your Lord, and He has already taken a covenant from you, if you are [truly] believers?" (وَ ما لَكُمْ لا تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَ الرَّسُولُ يَدْعُوكُمْ لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِرَبِّكُمْ وَ قَدْ أَخَذَ مِيثاقَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ).

That is to say, if you are truly ready to accept the truth, its proofs are clear—both through fitrah (innate nature) and reason, and through transmitted evidence.

On one hand, the Prophet of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله has come to you with clear proofs and brilliant signs and miracles. On the other hand, Allah, by showing His signs in the universe and within your own beings, has taken a kind of existential covenant from you to believe in Him. Yet, you pay no heed to your innate nature and reason, nor do you attend to the matter of revelation. It becomes clear that you are not willing to believe at all, and that ignorance, prejudice, and blind imitation have dominated your thinking.

From what we have said, it becomes clear that the meaning of the phrase "if you are [truly] believers" is: "If you are ready to believe in anything and accept any proof, this is the place for it," because its proofs are manifest from every perspective.

The point here is that they saw the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself, heard his invitation without any intermediary, and witnessed his miracles with their own eyes; what excuse could possibly remain?

Thus, we read in a hadith: One day the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said to his companions:

"Which of the believers has the most amazing faith in your view?" They said: "The angels!" He said: "And why should they not believe when they are with their Lord?" They said: "Then the Prophets!" He said: "And why should they not believe when revelation descends upon them?" They said: "Then us!" He said: "And why should you not believe while I am among you? (At this point, everyone remained silent, and the Prophet said:) But the most amazing of believers in faith are a people who come after you; they find pages [of scripture] and believe in what is in them." [1]

And this is a reality: those who come into existence many years after the passing of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and only see the traces of the Messenger of Allah in books, yet realize the truth of his invitation, possess a great distinction over others.

The term "mithaq" (solemn covenant) can refer to the monotheistic nature (fitrah) or the rational proofs that appear to man upon observing the order of creation. The expression "birabbikum" (in your Lord), which points to Divine governance in the world of creation, is a witness to this meaning.

Some have considered "mithaq" here to refer to the covenant of the "World of Particles" (Alam al-Dharr), but this meaning seems unlikely except according to the same interpretation we previously mentioned for the World of Particles. [2]

The next verse, for further emphasis and explanation of this same meaning, adds: "He is the One who sends down clear signs upon His servant, to bring you out of the darknesses [of polytheism and ignorance] into the light [of faith and knowledge], and indeed Allah is Kind and Merciful to you" (هُوَ الَّذِي يُنَزِّلُ عَلى‌ عَبْدِهِ آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ لِيُخْرِجَكُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ وَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ بِكُمْ لَرَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ).

A group has interpreted "clear signs" here as all "miracles," and another group as the Quran. However, the concept of the verse is broad and includes all of these, although the expression "sending down" is most suitable for the Quran—the same Quran that tears through the veils of the darkness of disbelief, misguidance, and ignorance, and causes the sun of faith and awareness to rise within the human soul.

The expression "Kind and Merciful" (Ra'uf Rahim) is a subtle allusion to the fact that this emphatic and passionate Divine invitation toward faith and spending is a manifestation of Divine mercy that has come to all of you, and all its blessings in this world and the next return to yourselves.

As to whether there is a difference between "Ra'uf" and "Rahim" and what that difference is, there is discussion among commentators. The most appropriate view is that "Ra'uf" refers to His special love and grace toward the obedient, while "Rahim" refers to His mercy regarding the sinners.

Some have also said that "Ra'fat" (Kindness) is used for mercy before its manifestation, while "Rahmah" (Mercy) is a term applied to it after its manifestation.

Then, He proceeds to an argument for the issue of spending, saying: "And what is the matter with you that you do not spend in the way of Allah, while to Allah belongs the inheritance of the heavens and the earth?" (وَ ما لَكُمْ أَلَّا تُنْفِقُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَ لِلَّهِ مِيراثُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ).

That is to say, eventually all of you will close your eyes to the world and its blessings, leaving everything behind and departing. So now that it is in your possession, why do you not take your benefit?! "Mirath" (inheritance) originally—as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat—means wealth that is transferred to a person without a contract or the like, and what is transferred from the deceased to the survivors is one of its instances, which, due to frequent use, is the meaning that comes to mind when this word is mentioned.

The expression "to Allah belongs the inheritance of the heavens and the earth" is because not only the wealth and riches on earth, but whatever exists in all the heavens and the earth, returns to His Pure Essence. All creatures die, and Allah is the Heir to them all.

And since spending in different conditions and circumstances has different values, He adds in the next sentence: "Those of you who spent before the 'Victory' and fought are not equal [to those who did so after]" (لا يَسْتَوِي مِنْكُمْ مَنْ أَنْفَقَ مِنْ قَبْلِ الْفَتْحِ وَ قاتَلَ). [1]

There is discussion among commentators as to which "Victory" (فتح) is meant here. Some consider it an allusion to the "Conquest of Mecca" in the eighth year of the Hijrah, and some consider it an allusion to the "Victory of Hudaybiyyah" in the sixth year.

Of course, considering that the word "فتح" in the Surah "Indeed, We have granted you a clear victory" is interpreted as the Victory of Hudaybiyyah, it is appropriate that it be the Victory of Hudaybiyyah here as well. However, the expression "fought" (قاتل) is more suitable for the Conquest of Mecca, because no fighting occurred at Hudaybiyyah, whereas in the Conquest of Mecca, a swift and short battle took place that did not face much resistance.

The possibility also exists that "the Victory" in this verse refers to the genus of victory and any kind of triumph for Muslims in Islamic wars. That is: those who did not withhold their wealth and lives during critical times are superior to those who rushed to the aid of Islam after the storms had subsided.

Therefore, for further emphasis, He adds: "Those are greater in degree than those who spent afterwards and fought" (أُولئِكَ أَعْظَمُ دَرَجَةً مِنَ الَّذِينَ أَنْفَقُوا مِنْ بَعْدُ وَ قاتَلُوا).

It is strange that a group of commentators who consider the verse to refer to the Conquest of Mecca or Hudaybiyyah have identified the instance of the spender in this verse as Abu Bakr. This is while, undoubtedly, from the time of the Hijrah until that time—which lasted 6 to 8 years and saw many battles—thousands of people spent in the way of Islam and fought. For in the Conquest of Mecca, according to histories, ten thousand people participated, and certainly a large number of this group provided financial aid to the war programs and spent in the way of Allah. It is clear that the expression "before" means spending on the eve of this victory, not at the beginning of Islam twenty-one years prior!

It is also necessary to mention that some commentators insist that spending is superior to Jihad to align with their preconceptions, perhaps taking the mention of spending before Jihad in the above verse as evidence for this. However, it is clear that placing financial spending first is because the means, preliminaries, and tools of war are provided through it; otherwise, undoubtedly, sacrificing one's life and readiness for martyrdom is superior to and higher than financial spending.

In any case, since both groups, despite the difference in degree, are included in Divine favors, He adds at the end of the verse: "And to each Allah has promised the best [reward]" (وَ كُلًّا وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الْحُسْنى‌).

This is an appreciation for all those who stepped on this path, and "al-Husna" (the best) here has a broad meaning that encompasses every kind of good reward in this world and the Hereafter.

And since the value of an action lies in its sincerity, He adds at the end of the verse: "And Allah is Aware of what you do" (وَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ). He is aware of both the quantity and quality of your actions, as well as your intentions and the degree of your sincerity.

In the last verse under discussion, again to encourage "spending in the way of Allah," He uses another interesting expression, saying: "Who is it that will lend to Allah a goodly loan, and spend from the wealth He has granted him, so that He may multiply it for him? And for him is a noble reward" (مَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يُقْرِضُ اللَّهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً فَيُضاعِفَهُ لَهُ وَ لَهُ أَجْرٌ كَرِيمٌ).

Truly, it is a wondrous expression! God, who is the Giver of all blessings, and every particle of our existence benefits moment by moment from the endless sea of His grace and is His property, has counted us as owners of wealth and has come to seek a loan from us. Unlike ordinary loans where the exact same amount is returned, He multiplies it several times—sometimes hundreds and sometimes thousands of times—and in addition to this, He promises a "noble reward," which is a magnificent reward that none but God knows.

Key Points

1- Motives for Spending

It is noteworthy that in the preceding verses, to encourage spending—whether helping the cause of Jihad or other spending for the needy—various expressions are seen, each of which can be a factor for movement toward this goal.

In the seventh verse, there is a reference to the issue of "istikhlaf" (succession) of people from one another, or from Allah in these riches, which considers true ownership as belonging exclusively to Allah and everyone as His representative in this wealth. This vision can open a person's hand and heart in spending and be a factor for movement in this field.

In the tenth verse, another expression appears that speaks of the instability of wealth and assets and their remaining after all humans have gone, and that is the expression "inheritance" (mirath). It says the inheritance of the heavens and the earth belongs to Allah.

And in the eleventh verse, the expression is the most sensitive of all: it counts Allah as the borrower and humans as the lenders—a loan in which the issue of the prohibition of usury does not apply, and several times up to thousands of times is paid in return, in addition to a magnificent reward that no mind can conceive!

All of this is to eliminate deviant visions and the motives of greed, avarice, envy, selfishness, and long hopes that prevent spending, and to build a society based on emotional bonds, social spirit, and deep cooperation.

2- Conditions for Spending in the Way of Allah

The expression "a goodly loan" (qardan hasanan) in the above verse is an allusion to the fact that lending itself has various types, some of which can be considered a "goodly loan" and some as low-value or even worthless loans.

The Holy Quran has stated the conditions for a goodly loan to Allah—or in other words, "valuable spending"—in various verses, and some commentators have derived ten conditions from their collection:

  1. It should be chosen from the best part of the wealth, not from low-value assets: "O you who have believed, spend from the good things which you have earned and from that which We have produced for you from the earth. And do not aim toward the defective therefrom, spending [from that] while you would not take it [yourselves] except with closed eyes. And know that Allah is Free of need and Praiseworthy" (Baqarah: 267).

  2. It should be from wealth that the person himself needs, as He says: "And they give [them] preference over themselves, even though they are in privation" (Hashr: 9).

  3. One should spend on those who are in dire need of it and consider priorities: "[Charity is] for the poor who have been restricted for the cause of Allah" (Baqarah: 273).

  4. It is better if the spending is concealed: "And if you hide them and give them to the poor, it is better for you" (Baqarah: 271).

  5. It should never be accompanied by reminders of generosity or injury: "O you who have believed, do not invalidate your charities with reminders or injury" (Baqarah: 264).

  6. Spending must be coupled with sincerity and pure intention: "[Those who] spend their wealth seeking the pleasure of Allah" (Baqarah: 265).

  7. One should consider what they spend as small and insignificant, even if it is outwardly large: "And do not confer favor to acquire more" (Muddaththir: 6). [1]

  8. It should be from wealth that one is attached to and loves: "Never will you attain the good [reward] until you spend [in the way of Allah] from that which you love" (Al-Imran: 92).

  9. One should never imagine themselves as the true owner, but rather see themselves as an intermediary between the Creator and the creation: "And spend out of that in which He has made you successors" (Hadid: 7).

  10. And before everything, the spending must be from lawful (halal) wealth, for Allah only accepts that: "Indeed, Allah only accepts from the righteous [who fear Him]" (Ma'idah: 27).

And it is mentioned in a hadith that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Allah does not accept charity from [wealth gained through] betrayal." [2]

What has been said is, in fact, a significant part of the necessary attributes and conditions, but it is not limited to these. With careful reflection on Islamic verses and narrations, other conditions can also be found.

Furthermore, what has been mentioned includes some conditions that are considered "obligatory" (such as the absence of reminders of favor, injury, and showing off) and some that are conditions of perfection (such as self-sacrifice at the time of one's own need), the absence of which does not destroy the value of the spending, though it is not at the highest level.

Also, what has been said, although regarding spending (a loan to Allah), many of them are also true regarding ordinary loans and are, so to speak, necessary conditions or conditions of perfection for "Qard al-Hasana."

Regarding the importance of "spending in the way of Allah," we have provided a detailed explanation under verses 261 to 267 of Surah Baqarah (Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 232 to 257).

3- Pioneers in Faith, Jihad, and Spending

Undoubtedly, those who precede others in faith and good deeds possess both more courage and awareness, as well as greater altruism and sacrifice. For this reason, they are not equal in the presence of Allah. Thus, the above verses emphasized this very issue: that those who spent and fought before the Victory (the Conquest of Mecca or Hudaybiyyah, or Islamic victories in general) are not equal to others in the presence of Allah.

In a hadith narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, he says: In the year of Hudaybiyyah (the sixth year of the Hijrah), we were with the Messenger of Allah. When we reached "Usfan" (a place near Mecca), he said: "In the future, a people may come who will consider your deeds small in comparison to their own deeds!"

We said: "Who are they, O Messenger of Allah! Do you mean the Quraysh?"

He said: "No! They are the people of Yemen, who have hearts more tender and soft than yours (and more deeds)."

We said: "Are they better than us, O Messenger of Allah?!" He said: "If one of them had a mountain of gold and spent it in the way of Allah, it would not equal a mudd [1] or half of it that you spend. Know that this is a difference between us and the people, and the witness to it is this word of Allah: 'Those of you who spent before the Victory and fought are not equal...'" [2]

It is also noteworthy that the meaning of lending to the Lord is any kind of spending in His way, one of the important instances of which is helping the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imam of the Muslims so that he may use it for the necessary expenses of administering the Islamic government.

Therefore, in a narration in Al-Kafi from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated that he said: "Allah did not ask His creation for a loan from what is in their hands out of a need for that; whatever right belongs to Allah is for His Wali (representative)." [3]

And in another hadith from Imam al-Kadhim عليه السلام under the verse in question (Who is it that will lend to Allah...), it is stated: "This verse was revealed regarding the gift and aid to the Imam." [4]

[^1]: Apparently, it refers to a mudd of food (less than one kilogram). [^2]: Al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 172. [^3]: Tafsir al-Safi, p. 522. [^4]: Tafsir al-Safi, p. 522.

Ayahs

يَوْمَ تَرَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ يَسْعَىٰ نُورُهُم بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمَـٰنِهِم بُشْرَىٰكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ جَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (١٢)
The day you will see the faithful, men and women, with their light moving swiftly in front of them and on their right, [and they are greeted with the words:] ‘There is good news for you today! Gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever]. That is the great success.’ (12)
يَوْمَ يَقُولُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتُ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱنظُرُونَا نَقْتَبِسْ مِن نُّورِكُمْ قِيلَ ٱرْجِعُوا۟ وَرَآءَكُمْ فَٱلْتَمِسُوا۟ نُورًا فَضُرِبَ بَيْنَهُم بِسُورٍ لَّهُۥ بَابٌۢ بَاطِنُهُۥ فِيهِ ٱلرَّحْمَةُ وَظَـٰهِرُهُۥ مِن قِبَلِهِ ٱلْعَذَابُ (١٣)
The day the hypocrites, men and women, will say to the faithful, ‘Please wait, that we may glean something from your light!’ They will be told: ‘Go back and grope for light!’ Then there will be set up between them a wall with a gate, with mercy within and punishment without. (13)
يُنَادُونَهُمْ أَلَمْ نَكُن مَّعَكُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ وَلَـٰكِنَّكُمْ فَتَنتُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَتَرَبَّصْتُمْ وَٱرْتَبْتُمْ وَغَرَّتْكُمُ ٱلْأَمَانِىُّ حَتَّىٰ جَآءَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ وَغَرَّكُم بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْغَرُورُ (١٤)
They will call out to them, ‘Did we not use to be with you?’ They will say, ‘Yes! But you cast yourselves into perdition. You awaited and were skeptical, and [false] hopes deceived you until the edict of Allah came, and the Deceiver deceived you concerning Allah. (14)
فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنكُمْ فِدْيَةٌ وَلَا مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ مَأْوَىٰكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ هِىَ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٥)
So today no ransom shall be taken from you or the faithless. The Fire will be your abode: it is your [ultimate] refuge and an evil destination.’ (15)

Commentary

Let Us Also Benefit From Your Light!

Since Allah promised a "generous reward" (Ajr Karim) to those who spend in His cause in the final verse of the preceding section, the verses under discussion clarify on which day this generous, valuable, and magnificent reward will be granted.

It says: "On the Day you see the believing men and believing women, their light proceeding before them and on their right" (يَوْمَ تَرَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَ الْمُؤْمِناتِ يَسْعى‌ نُورُهُمْ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ بِأَيْمانِهِمْ‌).

Although the addressee here is the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, others will certainly witness this scene as well. However, since identifying the believers is necessary for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله so he may show them greater favor, he recognizes them perfectly by this sign.

While commentators have offered various possibilities regarding this "light," it is clear that it refers to the personification of the light of faith, as the expression used is "their light" (the light of the believing men and women). This is not surprising, for on that Day, the beliefs and actions of human beings will take physical form. Faith, which is the light of guidance, will be personified as physical radiance and light, while disbelief, which is absolute darkness, will be personified as physical gloom.

Hence, we read in Surah At-Tahrim, verse 8: "On the Day that Allah will not disgrace the Prophet and those who believed with him. Their light will proceed before them" (يَوْمَ لا يُخْزِي اللَّهُ النَّبِيَّ وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ نُورُهُمْ يَسْعى‌ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ‌).

In other verses of the Quran, it is also mentioned that Allah "brings the believers out of darkness into the light."

The use of the word "yas‘a" (from the root sa‘y, meaning rapid movement) indicates that the believers themselves are swiftly traversing the path of the Gathering toward Paradise and the center of eternal happiness, as the rapid movement of their light is inseparable from their own rapid movement.

It is noteworthy that only two lights are mentioned: the light moving in front of the believers and the light on their right. This expression might refer to two different groups of believers: the "Foremost" (Muqarrabun) whose faces are radiant and whose light moves before them, and the "Companions of the Right" (Ashab al-Yamin) whose light is on their right side because their record of deeds is given to their right hand, and light emanates from it. It is also possible that both refer to a single group, where the "light of the right" is a metaphor for the light arising from their righteous deeds, illuminating their entire surroundings.

In any case, this light is their guide toward the loftiest Paradise, and in its radiance, they swiftly traverse the path to Heaven.

Furthermore, there is no doubt that this divine light, arising from faith and righteous deeds, varies according to the levels of people's faith and actions. Those with stronger faith will have a light that illuminates a vast distance, while those at a lower level will possess less light, to the point that for some, the light only illuminates the tips of their toes! As mentioned in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim regarding the verse under discussion: "The light will be distributed among the people on the Day of Resurrection according to the measure of their faith" [1]. It is at this moment, out of respect for them, that the call of the angels arises: "Glad tidings for you today! Gardens of Paradise beneath which rivers flow" (بُشْراكُمُ الْيَوْمَ جَنَّاتٌ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ).

"You shall abide therein forever; that is the great success" (خالِدِينَ فِيها ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ‌).

However, the hypocrites, who are situated in the terrifying darkness of disbelief, hypocrisy, and sin, will cry out at this moment, pleading with the believers for light.

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 241, Hadith 60.

But they hear nothing but a negative response, as the next verse says: "On the Day the hypocrite men and hypocrite women will say to those who believed: 'Wait for us (or look at us) that we may acquire a spark from your light'" (يَوْمَ يَقُولُ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَ الْمُنافِقاتُ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا انْظُرُونا نَقْتَبِسْ مِنْ نُورِكُمْ‌). [1] The word iqtibas originally comes from the root qabas, meaning to take a flame from a fire, and was later applied to taking other types of samples or benefits.

The meaning of the phrase "unzuruna" is "look at us" so that we may benefit from the light of your faces and find our way, or "cast a look of grace and kindness upon us and give us a share of your light." It is also possible that "unzuruna" means "wait for us," i.e., give us a little time so we can catch up to you and find the way in the radiance of your light.

In any case, the response given to them is: "Go back behind you and seek light!" (قِيلَ ارْجِعُوا وَراءَكُمْ فَالْتَمِسُوا نُوراً).

This is not the place to acquire light; you should have obtained it from the world you left behind through faith and righteous deeds, but that is now in the past and it is too late! "Then a wall will be placed between them with a gate" (فَضُرِبَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِسُورٍ لَهُ بابٌ‌).

[1]: "Unzuruna" from the root nazar originally means thought or looking to observe or perceive something, and sometimes it means reflection and searching. When it is transitive with "ila," it means casting one's eyes toward something; when transitive with "fi," it means reflection and contemplation; and when it is transitive without a preposition, as in "nazartuhu," "anzartuhu," or "intazartuhu," it means to delay or to wait. (Adapted from Mufradat al-Raghib).

However, the two sides of this massive wall, or this gate, are completely different: "Its interior contains mercy, and its exterior faces punishment" (باطِنُهُ فِيهِ الرَّحْمَةُ وَ ظاهِرُهُ مِنْ قِبَلِهِ الْعَذابُ‌).

"Sur" linguistically refers to the wall that was built around cities in the past for protection (ramparts), which often had towers at various intervals for guards.

It is noteworthy that it says: "Its interior is mercy, and its exterior is punishment," meaning the believers are like the inhabitants of a city inside this garden, while the hypocrites are like outsiders in the desert area. Previously, they lived together in one society, but a massive wall of differing beliefs and actions separated them; on the Day of Resurrection, this same reality is personified.

But what is this "gate" for? It might be so that the hypocrites can see the blessings of Paradise through it and feel regret, or so that those who are less tainted may pass through it after being purified to join the believers.

However, this wall is not such that it prevents the passage of sound; therefore, the next verse adds: "They will call out to them: 'Were we not with you?'" (يُنادُونَهُمْ أَ لَمْ نَكُنْ مَعَكُمْ‌).

We lived with you in one society in the world, and we were beside you here; what happened that you were suddenly separated from us and went toward the Spirit and Divine Mercy, leaving us in the grip of punishment? "They will reply: 'Yes!'" (قالُوا بَلى‌).

We were together everywhere—in the streets and markets, in travel and at home; sometimes we were neighbors, or even lived in the same house, but in terms of school of thought, belief, and action, we were leagues apart. You separated your path from ours and were strangers to the Truth in both principles and applications.

Then they add: "But you fell into great errors, including:

  1. "You led yourselves into temptation (and destruction) by following the path of disbelief" (وَ لكِنَّكُمْ فَتَنْتُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ‌).

  2. "You were constantly waiting (for the death of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the destruction of the Muslims, and the uprooting of the foundations of Islam)" (وَ تَرَبَّصْتُمْ‌). Furthermore, you maintained a state of hesitation and procrastination regarding every positive deed and correct movement.

  3. "You were constantly in doubt regarding the Resurrection, the truth of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله call, and the Quran" (وَ ارْتَبْتُمْ‌).

  4. "You were always captivated by long-term desires—desires that never let go of you until Allah's command of your death arrived" (وَ غَرَّتْكُمُ الْأَمانِيُّ حَتَّى جاءَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ‌). Indeed, these desires did not give you a moment's chance for correct thinking; you were drowned in dreams and fancies, living in a world of illusion, dominated by the desire to reach lusts and material goals.

  5. "Beyond all this, the Deceiver (Satan, who had established his base within your being) deceived you concerning Allah" (وَ غَرَّكُمْ بِاللَّهِ الْغَرُورُ). He deceived you with his whispers; sometimes he made the world appear eternal to you, sometimes he portrayed the Hereafter as an empty promise, sometimes he made you overconfident in Allah's grace and mercy, and sometimes he questioned the very existence of the Great God! These five factors joined hands and completely separated your path from ours.

"Fatantum" from the root fitnah has various meanings: trial and testing, deceiving, affliction and punishment, misguidance, and polytheism. Here, the last two meanings—misguidance and polytheism—are most appropriate.

"Tarabbastum" from the root tarabbus originally means waiting, whether waiting for calamity or for abundance. Here, it most appropriately refers to waiting for the death of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the end of Islam, or waiting in the sense of procrastinating in repenting from sin and performing good deeds.

"Wartabtum" from the root rayb refers to any doubt that is later uncovered. Here, it is most suitable for doubt in the Resurrection or the truth of the Quran.

Although the concepts of the words used in the verse are broad, they may sequentially represent the issues of "polytheism," waiting for "the end of Islam and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله," then doubt in the "Resurrection," followed by practical pollutions through the deception of "false hopes" and "Satan." Thus, the first three phrases refer to the three principles of religion (Usul al-Din), and the last two refer to the branches of religion (Furu' al-Din).

Finally, the believers address the hypocrites with a concluding result, saying: "So today no ransom shall be taken from you to save you from Allah's punishment" (فَالْيَوْمَ لا يُؤْخَذُ مِنْكُمْ فِدْيَةٌ).

"Nor from those who disbelieved" (وَ لا مِنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا). Thus, the disbelievers share the same fate as the hypocrites; all are held hostage by their sins and the ugliness of their deeds, with no way of escape.

Then they add: "Your abode is the Fire" (مَأْواكُمُ النَّارُ). And "your guardian (and what is most appropriate for you) is Hell" (هِيَ مَوْلاكُمْ‌) [1] "and what an evil destination it is!" (وَ بِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ).

Usually, to escape punishment in the world, humans resort to financial compensation or seek help from a supporter or intercessor, but there, neither of these exists for the hypocrites and disbelievers.

In principle, on the Day of Resurrection, all material means and instruments common in this world for reaching goals will cease to function, and ties will be severed, as we read in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 166: "And all relations between them are cut off" (وَ تَقَطَّعَتْ بِهِمُ الْأَسْبابُ‌).

"A Day in which there is no exchange and no friendship" (يَوْمٌ لا بَيْعٌ فِيهِ وَ لا خُلَّةٌ) (Baqarah 254). "Nor shall any compensation be accepted from it" (وَ لا يُؤْخَذُ مِنْها عَدْلٌ‌) (Baqarah 48). "A Day when no friend shall avail a friend in anything" (يَوْمَ لا يُغْنِي مَوْلًى عَنْ مَوْلًى شَيْئاً) (Dukhan 41). "Nor will their plotting avail them anything" (يَوْمَ لا يُغْنِي عَنْهُمْ كَيْدُهُمْ شَيْئاً) (Tur 45). "And there will be no kinship between them that Day" (فلا أَنْسابَ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ) (Mu'minun 101). "In short, every soul is a hostage for what it has earned" (كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ) (Muddathir 38).

Thus, the Quran clarifies that the only means of salvation on that Day is faith and righteous deeds. Even the circle of intercession is limited to those who possess a share of these two, not absolute strangers and those who have completely severed their ties with God and the Saints of Allah.

Key Point: The Futile Pleas of Criminals on the Day of Resurrection

Since many people, upon entering the arena of the Gathering, are unfamiliar with the system governing it, they believe that the systems of the world also rule there. Therefore, they resort to them, but they soon realize what a great mistake they have made.

Sometimes the criminals seek help from the believers, saying: "Look at us that we may acquire a spark from your light" (the verses under discussion), but they are soon met with a negative response that the source of light is not here; it was in the life of the world, which you passed through heedlessly.

Sometimes the criminals seek help from one another (the followers from their leaders), saying: "Can you then avail us against anything of the punishment of Allah?" (Ibrahim 21), where they are also met with a negative response.

Sometimes they even plead with the keepers of Hell, saying: "Call upon your Lord to lighten for us a day of the punishment" (Mu'min 49).

And sometimes they go even further, seeking help from Allah, saying: "Our Lord! Bring us out of this; then if we return (to evil), we shall indeed be wrongdoers" (Mu'minun 107). But it is clear that this path is also closed, for the period of obligation has ended, and that is the realm of reward and punishment.

[1]: "Mawla" here may mean guardian and protector, or it may mean the person or thing that has priority for a person.

Ayahs

۞ أَلَمْ يَأْنِ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَن تَخْشَعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ لِذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا نَزَلَ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا يَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلُ فَطَالَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْأَمَدُ فَقَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ ۖ وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ (١٦)
Is it not time yet for those who have faith that their hearts should be humbled for Allah’s remembrance and toward the truth which has come down [to them], and to be not like those who were given the Book before? Time took its toll on them and so their hearts were hardened, and many of them are transgressors. (16)
ٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحْىِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا ۚ قَدْ بَيَّنَّا لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (١٧)
Know that Allah revives the earth after its death. We have certainly made the signs clear for you, so that you may exercise your reason. (17)
إِنَّ ٱلْمُصَّدِّقِينَ وَٱلْمُصَّدِّقَـٰتِ وَأَقْرَضُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا يُضَـٰعَفُ لَهُمْ وَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ كَرِيمٌ (١٨)
Indeed the charitable men and women and those who lend Allah a good loan—it shall be multiplied for them, and there will be a noble reward for them. (18)

Occasion of Revelation

Several occasions of revelation have been narrated for the first verse under discussion, including the following:

The aforementioned verse was revealed one year after the Hijrah concerning the hypocrites. This was because one day they asked Salman al-Farsi, "Tell us about what is in the Torah! For in the Torah, there are wondrous matters" (and by this means, they intended to show indifference toward the Quran). At that moment, the opening verses of Surah Yusuf were revealed. Salman said to them, "This Quran is 'the best of stories' (احسن القصص) and the finest of narratives, and it is more beneficial for you than anything else."

For a while, they refrained from repeating their request, but then they came to Salman again and repeated the same desire. At that moment, the verse was revealed: اللَّهُ نَزَّلَ أَحْسَنَ الْحَدِيثِ كِتاباً مُتَشابِهاً مَثانِيَ تَقْشَعِرُّ مِنْهُ جُلُودُ الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ‌... "Allah has sent down the best statement: a consistent Book wherein is reiteration. The shivers from it the skins of those who fear their Lord..." (Az-Zumar: 23).

Again, they refrained from repeating this question for a time, and for the third time, they came to Salman and repeated the same request. At this point, the verse under discussion was revealed (reproaching them: has the time not come for you to humble yourselves before the name of Allah and abandon such talk?).

In another occasion of revelation, it is mentioned that the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in Mecca lived through drought and a difficult life. When they migrated and reached abundance and blessings, their condition changed, and hardness overcame the hearts of some of them, whereas it was necessary for their faith, certainty, and sincerity to increase due to their companionship with the Quran. (The above verse was revealed and warned them) [1]. Some other occasions of revelation are also seen regarding this verse, but since they suggest the verse is Meccan, they are not reliable, as the famous view is that this entire Surah was revealed in Medina.

Commentary

How Long Will Negligence and Heedlessness Last?!

After mentioning all those crushing warnings and awakening alerts in the previous verses, and describing the painful fate of the hypocrites and disbelievers on the Day of Resurrection, the first verse under discussion states as a conclusion: "Has the time not come for those who have believed that their hearts should become humble and submissive at the remembrance of Allah and what has come down of the truth?

And that they be not like those who were given the Scripture before (like the Jews and Christians), then a long period passed over them and they forgot Allah, and their hearts became hardened, and many of them were defiant and sinful" (أَ لَمْ يَأْنِ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ تَخْشَعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ لِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ وَ ما نَزَلَ مِنَ الْحَقِّ وَ لا يَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَطالَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَمَدُ فَقَسَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَ كَثِيرٌ مِنْهُمْ فاسِقُونَ‌) [2].

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 237. In the commentary Durr al-Manthur, occasions of revelation similar to the second one are also mentioned (Vol. 6, p. 175). Baydawi also mentioned this in his commentary (Anwar al-Tanzil).

[2]: "Ya'ni" (يأن) is from the root ana (أنى), meaning the approaching or arrival of the time for something. "Takhsha'a" (تخشع) is from khushu', meaning a state of humility and physical and spiritual etiquette that overcomes a person in the presence of an important truth or a great personality.

It is clear that if the remembrance of Allah resides in the depths of the soul, and likewise, if the verses revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله are properly contemplated, they must cause humility. However, the Quran severely blames a group of believers here: why do they not become humble before these matters? And why have they fallen into negligence and heedlessness like many previous nations? That same negligence whose result is hardness of heart, and that hardness whose fruit is defiance and sin! Is it consistent with faith to be content only with the claim of belief, to pass over these important matters simply, and to surrender to a comfortable life, living in luxury and blessings, and being constantly occupied with pleasure? The phrase "the term was prolonged for them" (طال عليهم الامد) might refer to the time interval between them and their prophets, or long life and long-held desires, or the non-descent of divine punishment for a long time, or all of these; for each can be a factor for negligence and hardness, which in turn is a cause for defiance and sin.

In a Hadith from Ali عليه السلام, we read: "Do not rush a matter before its time arrives, lest you regret it, and let not the term be prolonged for you, lest your hearts harden." [1]

In another Hadith from the tongue of Prophet Jesus عليه السلام, we read: "Do not speak much without the remembrance of Allah, for it causes hardness of heart, and the hardened heart is far from Allah. And do not look at the sins of the servants as if you were lords; rather, look at your own sins as if you were servants. People are of two types: the afflicted and the healthy. So have mercy on the people of affliction, and praise Allah for health." [2]

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 78, p. 83, Hadith 85. [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 238.

Since the reviving of dead hearts through divine remembrance and finding spiritual life in the light of humility and submission before the Quran bears a great resemblance to the reviving of dead lands by the blessing of life-giving raindrops, the next verse adds: "Know that Allah gives life to the earth after its death" (اعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ يُحْيِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها). "We have made clear the signs to you in the scene of creation and also in the scene of revelation, that perhaps you will reason" (قَدْ بَيَّنَّا لَكُمُ الْآياتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ‌).

In truth, this verse is both an allusion to the reviving of dead lands by rain and the reviving of dead hearts by the remembrance of Allah and the Holy Quran, which descended from the heaven of revelation upon the pure heart of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, and both are worthy of contemplation and reasoning.

Therefore, Islamic narrations have pointed to both:

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that in interpreting this verse he said: "It means justice after injustice." [1]

And in another Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read regarding the interpretation of "Know that Allah gives life to the earth after its death": "Allah Almighty gives life to it through the Qaim [Imam al-Mahdi] after its death; meaning by its death, the disbelief of its inhabitants, for the disbeliever is dead!" [2]

It goes without saying that these interpretations are, in fact, expressions of the clear applications of the verse and never limit the concept of the verse.

In another Hadith from Imam al-Kadhim عليه السلام, it is stated: "For Allah gives life to dead hearts with the light of wisdom just as He gives life to the dead earth with heavy rain." [3]

In the next verse, it returns once more to the issue of spending (infaq), which is among the fruits of the tree of faith and humility, and repeats the same expression we read in previous verses with additions, saying: "Indeed, the men who practice charity and the women who practice charity and [they who] have lent Allah a goodly loan—it will be multiplied for them, and they will have a noble reward" (إِنَّ الْمُصَّدِّقِينَ وَ الْمُصَّدِّقاتِ وَ أَقْرَضُوا اللَّهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً يُضاعَفُ لَهُمْ وَ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ كَرِيمٌ‌) [4].

[1]: Rawdah al-Kafi, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 243. [2]: Kamal al-Din, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 242. [3]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 78, p. 308. [4]: "Al-Mussaddiqin" and "Al-Mussaddiqat" mean those who give charity.

Regarding why the issue of "spending" is presented as a "goodly loan" to Allah, and also what the multiplied reward and noble prize are for, we discussed this under verse 11 of this same Surah.

Some have suggested that "a goodly loan to Allah" in this verse and similar verses [1] means lending to the servants, because God has no need for a loan; it is the believing servants who need loans. However, considering the context of the verses, it seems that "goodly loan" in all these verses refers to "spending in the way of Allah," although lending to the servants of God is also among the most virtuous of deeds and there is no doubt in that.

Fadhil Miqdad also pointed to this meaning in Kanz al-'Irfan, although he interprets it to include all righteous deeds [2].

Key Point: Sinful Individuals Who Repented Upon Hearing This Verse

The verse "Has the time not come for those who have believed..." is among the soul-shaking verses of the Holy Quran that takes possession of the human heart and spirit, tears through the veils of negligence, and cries out: Has the time not come for believing hearts to be humble before the remembrance of Allah and what has been revealed of the truth? And that they should not be like those who received the verses of the Heavenly Book before them, but over the length of time, their hearts turned to hardness?

Thus, throughout history, we see very tainted individuals who, upon hearing this verse, were so shaken that in a single moment they bid farewell to all their sins, and some even joined the ranks of the ascetics and worshippers. Among these is the famous story of Fudhayl ibn 'Iyadh.

"Fudhayl," who is introduced in books of biography as one of the trustworthy narrators from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام and as a famous ascetic, and who lived near the Kaaba at the end of his life and passed away there on the day of Ashura, was in the beginning a dangerous highway robber whom everyone feared.

He was passing near a settlement when he saw a young girl and became attracted to her. A burning passion for the girl compelled Fudhayl to climb the wall of her house at night, intending to reach her at any cost.
