It was at this moment that in one of the nearby houses, an awake-hearted person was reciting the Quran and had reached this very verse: أَ لَمْ يَأْنِ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ تَخْشَعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ لِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ‌... This verse struck Fudhayl's tainted heart like an arrow. He felt a pain and burning within his soul and was strangely shaken. He thought for a moment: Who is this speaking? And to whom is he giving this message? He is saying to me: O Fudhayl! "Has the time not come for you to wake up, to return from this wrong path, to wash yourself of this pollution, and to grasp the mantle of repentance?!" Suddenly, Fudhayl's voice rose, and he kept saying: "Yes, by Allah, it has come! Yes, by Allah, it has come!" He had made his final decision, and with a lightning-like leap, he jumped out of the ranks of the wretched and took his place in the ranks of the blessed. He turned back, descended from the wall, and entered a ruin where a group of travelers were. They were consulting with one another about moving toward a destination, saying, "Fudhayl and his gang are on the road; if we go, they will block our path and plunder our wealth!" Fudhayl was shaken and blamed himself severely, saying, "What an evil man I am! What wretchedness has come upon me? In the heart of the night, I have come out of my house with the intent of sin, while a group of Muslims have fled to the corner of this ruin out of fear of me!" He turned his face toward the sky and, with a repentant heart, uttered these words:

"O Allah, I have repented to You, and I make my repentance to You to be my dwelling near Your Sacred House! O God, I am in pain from my evil-doing, and I am in lamentation from my worthlessness. Cure my pain, O Curer of all pains! And O Pure and Sanctified from all defects! O You who are independent of my service! And O You who are undiminished by my betrayal! Forgive me by Your mercy, and release me, who am a prisoner of the bonds of my own desires, from this bond!" Allah answered his prayer and bestowed favors upon him. He returned from there and came toward "Mecca." He lived there for years as a neighbor [of the Kaaba] and became one of the saints!

The beggar of Your street is independent of the eight heavens, The prisoner of Your love is free from both worlds! [1]

Some commentators have narrated that one of the famous men of Basra said: I was passing along a path when suddenly I heard a cry. I followed it and saw a man fallen unconscious on the ground. I asked, "What is this?" They said, "He is an awake-hearted man; he heard a verse from the Quran and lost consciousness." I asked, "Which verse?" They said: أَ لَمْ يَأْنِ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ تَخْشَعَ قُلُوبُهُمْ لِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ‌...

Suddenly, that unconscious man, hearing our voices, regained consciousness and began to recite these poignant verses:

Has the time not come for the separation to end? And for the branch of the fragrant willow to smile? And for the restless lover who has melted and bent, Has the time not come for him to be wept over and pitied? I wrote with the water of longing within my ribs, A letter that tells of a beautiful, colorful, and interesting page.

Then he said: "It is difficult, it is difficult, difficult!" He said this and once more fell unconscious to the ground. We moved him, but we saw that he had surrendered his soul to the Creator! [1]

[1]: Adapted from Safinat al-Bihar (Vol. 2, p. 369), Ruh al-Bayan (Vol. 9, p. 365), and Tafsir al-Qurtubi (Vol. 9, p. 6421). [1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 27, p. 156.

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلصِّدِّيقُونَ ۖ وَٱلشُّهَدَآءُ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ وَنُورُهُمْ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (١٩)
Those who have faith in Allah and His apostles—it is they who are the truthful and witnesses with their Lord; they shall have their reward and their light. But as for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of hell. (19)
ٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ وَزِينَةٌ وَتَفَاخُرٌۢ بَيْنَكُمْ وَتَكَاثُرٌ فِى ٱلْأَمْوَٰلِ وَٱلْأَوْلَـٰدِ ۖ كَمَثَلِ غَيْثٍ أَعْجَبَ ٱلْكُفَّارَ نَبَاتُهُۥ ثُمَّ يَهِيجُ فَتَرَىٰهُ مُصْفَرًّا ثُمَّ يَكُونُ حُطَـٰمًا ۖ وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِضْوَٰنٌ ۚ وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْغُرُورِ (٢٠)
Know that the life of this world is mere diversion and play, glamour and mutual vainglory among you and rivalry for wealth and children—like rain, whose growth impresses the farmer. Then it withers and you see it turn yellow, then it becomes chaff. Whereas in the Hereafter there is forgiveness from Allah and His approval and a severe punishment. The life of this world is nothing but the wares of delusion. (20)

Commentary

Worldly Life is Nothing but the Enjoyment of Delusion!

In continuation of the discussion from the previous verses regarding the believers and their reward before God, the verses under study add: "And those who believe in Allah and His messengers—it is they who are the truthful (Siddiqin) and the witnesses (Shuhada) before their Lord" (وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَ رُسُلِهِ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الصِّدِّيقُونَ وَ الشُّهَداءُ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ‌).

"Siddiq" is an intensive form of "Sidq" (truthfulness) and refers to someone who is truthfulness personified—one whose knowledge confirms his speech, and who is a perfect example of sincerity.

"Shuhada" is the plural of "Shahid," derived from the root "Shuhud," meaning "presence accompanied by observation," whether with the physical eye or the eye of the heart. If a witness is called a "Shahid," it is because of their presence and observation of a scene, just as this term is applied to "martyrs in the way of Allah" because of their presence on the field of Jihad.

However, in the verse under discussion, it may mean "witnessing over deeds," as understood from other Quranic verses stating that prophets are witnesses over the deeds of their nations, and the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله is a witness over them and over the Islamic Ummah, and Muslims are also witnesses over the deeds of people. [^1] Therefore, the station of "Shuhada" (witnesses over deeds) is a high rank reserved for people of faith.

Some have also suggested that "Shuhada" here carries the same meaning as martyrs

[^1]: Refer to Volume 14 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 184, under verse 78 of Surah Al-Hajj, and Volume 3, page 391 (under verse 41 of Surah An-Nisa).

in the way of Allah; meaning that believers possess the reward of martyrs and are considered as such. Thus, we have a Hadith where someone came to Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام and said: "Pray to Allah to grant me martyrdom!" The Imam عليه السلام replied: "The believer is a martyr," and then he recited this verse: "And those who believe in Allah and His messengers..." [^1] Of course, combining both meanings is also possible, especially since in the Holy Quran, the terms "Shahid" and "Shuhada" are frequently applied to witnesses over deeds and the like.

In any case, Allah describes true believers here with two attributes:

First, the attribute of "Siddiq" (the Truthful), and second, "Shahid" (the Witness/Martyr). This indicates that the "believers" intended in this verse possess a high level of faith; otherwise, how could an ordinary believer claim these two attributes for themselves? [^2] Then He adds: "For them is their reward and their light" (لَهُمْ أَجْرُهُمْ وَ نُورُهُمْ‌).

This concise expression alludes to their magnificent reward and their extraordinary light.

[^1]: "Tafsir al-Ayyashi" as quoted in "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 244.

[^2]: According to the above commentary, the sentence "أُولئِكَ هُمُ الصِّدِّيقُونَ وَ الشُّهَداءُ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ‌" requires no omitted words (taqdir), and considers this group of believers to be the actual manifestation of "the Truthful and the Witnesses." However, some commentators believe they are like the Truthful and the Witnesses, not the actual ones—meaning they have their reward but not all their honors. They say the verse implies: "For them is the like of the reward of the Truthful and the Witnesses" (Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani and al-Mizan under the verses). Naturally, the referents of the pronouns "for them" and "their reward" would differ, but this interpretation does not align with the apparent meaning of the verse (reflect on this).

And at the end, He says: "But those who disbelieve and deny Our signs—those are the companions of Hellfire" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْجَحِيمِ‌).

This is so that by contrasting these two groups, the high station of the first group and the degradation and wretchedness of the second group become clear.

And since a high level of faith was mentioned for the first group, intense disbelief is mentioned for this group, which is why it is coupled with the denial of divine signs.

Since the love of the world is the source of every sin and the "root of all evil," the next verse presents a vivid depiction of the state of worldly life, its various stages, and the motives governing each stage, saying: "Know that the life of this world is but play and amusement and adornment and boasting among you and rivalry in increase of wealth and children" (اعْلَمُوا أَنَّمَا الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا لَعِبٌ وَ لَهْوٌ وَ زِينَةٌ وَ تَفاخُرٌ بَيْنَكُمْ وَ تَكاثُرٌ فِي الْأَمْوالِ وَ الْأَوْلادِ). Thus, "heedlessness," "amusement," "adornment," "boasting," and "rivalry in increase" constitute the five periods of human life.

First is the "period of childhood," where life is submerged in a halo of heedlessness, unawareness, and play (la'ib).

Then comes the stage of "adolescence," where amusement (lahw) replaces play. In this stage, the human seeks matters that distract him and keep him away from serious issues.

The third stage is "youth," characterized by passion, love, and the pursuit of adornment (zinah).

Passing this stage, the fourth stage arrives, and the feelings of "acquiring status and boasting" (tafakhur) are awakened in the human.

Finally, he reaches the fifth stage, where he thinks of increasing wealth and numbers and accumulating riches (takathur).

The initial stages are roughly defined by age, but the later stages vary greatly among individuals. Some, like the stage of accumulating wealth, continue until the end of life, though some believe each of these five periods takes eight years of a person's life, totaling forty years, at which point the human personality becomes fixed.

It is also entirely possible for some people's personalities to remain stuck in the first or second stage, thinking of "play, amusement, and showing off" until old age, or remaining in the stage of adornment-seeking, where their only thought until death is providing houses, vehicles, and decorative clothing. These are children in old age and elders with a child's spirit! Then, by mentioning an example, He visualizes the beginning and end of worldly life before human eyes, saying: "[It is] like the example of a rain whose [resulting] plant growth pleases the tillers; then it dries and you see it turned yellow; then it becomes [scattered] debris"! (كَمَثَلِ غَيْثٍ أَعْجَبَ الْكُفَّارَ نَباتُهُ ثُمَّ يَهِيجُ فَتَراهُ مُصْفَرًّا ثُمَّ يَكُونُ حُطاماً) [^1]

[^1]: "Yahiju" from the root "hayajan" has two meanings in linguistics: one is the drying of a plant, and the other is to be set in motion or agitation. It is possible these two meanings return to one root, because when a plant dries, it becomes ready for separation, scattering, movement, and agitation!

"Kuffar" here does not mean faithless individuals, but rather "farmers," because the original meaning of "kufr" is "to cover." Since the farmer sows the seed and covers it under the soil, he is called a "kafar." Thus, sometimes "kufr" is used to mean a grave because it covers the body of the deceased, and sometimes the night is called "kafar" because its darkness covers everything.

In fact, the verse under discussion is like verse 29 of Surah Al-Fath, which, when speaking of the abundant growth of a plant, says: "it pleases the sowers" (yu'jibu al-zurra')—meaning "sower" (zarra') is used instead of "kuffar."

Some commentators have also suggested that "kuffar" here refers to those who disbelieve in Allah, providing certain justifications, but this interpretation does not seem very appropriate, as both the believer and the disbeliever share in this amazement.

"Hutam" is from the root "hatm," meaning to break and crush, and the scattered pieces of straw that fly in every direction with a strong wind are called "hutam."

Indeed, the stages that a human traverses over seventy years or more appear in plants within a few months, and a human can sit by a field and witness the passing of life, its beginning and its end, in a brief observation.

Then, addressing the outcome of life and its final result and product, He adds: "But in the Hereafter there is either intense punishment or forgiveness from Allah and His pleasure" (وَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ وَ مَغْفِرَةٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ رِضْوانٌ‌).

And finally, He concludes the verse with this sentence: "And the worldly life is nothing but the enjoyment of delusion!" (وَ مَا الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا مَتاعُ الْغُرُورِ).

"Ghurur" is originally from the root "gharr" (rhyming with harr), meaning the apparent effect of something;

hence, the white mark on a horse's forehead is called "ghurrah." Later, it was applied to a state of heedlessness where a person appears alert on the outside but is actually unaware, and it is also used to mean deception and trickery.

"Mata'" means any kind of means for utilization. Therefore, the sentence "the world is the enjoyment of delusion" means it is a tool and instrument for deception—deceiving oneself and deceiving others. Of course, this applies to those who make the world their ultimate goal, attach their hearts to it, rely upon it, and whose final wish is to attain it. However, if the blessings of this material world are a means to reach high human values and eternal happiness, it is never "the world" [in a negative sense], but rather the "tilled field of the Hereafter" and a bridge to reach those great goals.

Clearly, viewing the world and its blessings as a "passageway" or a "permanent dwelling" gives a person two different orientations: in one, it is the cause of conflict, corruption, transgression, injustice, rebellion, and heedlessness; in the other, it is a means of justice, awareness, altruism, sacrifice, brotherhood, and forgiveness.

Key Points

1- The Station of the Truthful (Siddiqin) and the Witnesses (Shuhada)

In the Holy Quran, a group of great prophets and their like are described with the attribute of "Sidq" (truthfulness), including Ibrahim: "Indeed, he was a man of truth (Siddiq), a prophet" (Surah Maryam, verse 41). The same expression is used regarding "Idris," the great prophet of God (Maryam-56). Regarding "Maryam," the mother of Christ, we read: "And his mother was a woman of truth (Siddiqah)" (Al-Ma'idah 75).

In some verses of the Quran, the "Siddiqin" are mentioned alongside the prophets,

as we read in An-Nisa verse 69: "And whoever obeys Allah and the Messenger—those will be with the ones upon whom Allah has bestowed favor of the prophets, the steadfast affirmers of truth (Siddiqin), the martyrs (Shuhada) and the righteous (Salihin). And excellent are those as companions."

As we said, this word is an intensive form of the root "Sidq" and is applied to someone whose entire being is encompassed by truth and honesty, and whose sincerity is reflected in their thoughts, words, deeds, and entire life. This shows the importance of the station of truthfulness.

As for "Shuhada," as we mentioned, it sometimes means witnesses over deeds and sometimes martyrs in the way of Allah, and in the verse under discussion, combining both is possible.

Of course, "Shahid" in Islamic culture is not limited to those killed on the field of Jihad, although they are the clearest manifestation of it. Rather, all those who hold the true belief, walk the path of truth, and pass away in this path are, according to Islamic narrations, among the martyrs.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read that he said:

"The one among you who knows this matter (Wilayah), awaits it, and seeks reward in it, is like one who fought, by Allah, alongside the Qa'im of the family of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله with his sword." Then he said: "Nay, by Allah, like one who fought alongside the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله with his sword." Then he said a third time: "Nay, by Allah, like one who was martyred with the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله in his tent! And regarding you there is a verse in the Book of Allah." I said: "And which verse, may I be sacrificed for you?" He said: "The words of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: 'And those who believe in Allah and His messengers—it is they who are the truthful and the witnesses before their Lord...' Then he said: 'By Allah, you have become the truthful and the witnesses before your Lord.'" [^1] We conclude this with a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام: when a group of his companions were impatient for the time of Jihad and martyrdom in the way of Allah, he said:

"Do not hasten for that which Allah has not hastened for you, for whoever among you dies in his bed while possessing the knowledge of the right of his Lord and the right of His Messenger and his Household (Ahl al-Bayt), dies a martyr!" [^2]

2- Worldly Life is a Collection of These Motives

In various verses of the Quran, worldly life is sometimes described as "play and amusement" (la'ib wa lahw), such as: "And the worldly life is nothing but play and amusement" (Al-An'am-32).

[^1]: "Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 238.

[^2]: "Nahj al-Balagha," Sermon 190.

And sometimes it is described as "play, amusement, adornment, boasting, and rivalry in increase" (as in the verses under study). Sometimes it is referred to as "the enjoyment of delusion" (as in Al-Imran-185 and the verses under study), and sometimes as "a brief enjoyment" (An-Nisa-77).

And sometimes as a transient, external, and fleeting matter (An-Nisa-94).

From the collection of these expressions and other Quranic terms, the Islamic perspective on material life and its blessings becomes clearly evident: it assigns them a negligible value, and considers the inclination and attachment to them as arising from aimless movements (play), distracting goals (amusement), pursuit of luxury (adornment), love of status, leadership, and superiority over others (boasting), and greed and rivalry in increase (takathur). It considers the love for it to be the source of all kinds of injustices and sins.

However, if these material blessings change direction and become a ladder to reach divine goals, they become assets that Allah buys from the believers, granting them eternal Paradise and everlasting happiness: "Indeed, Allah has purchased from the believers their lives and their properties [in exchange] for that they will have Paradise..." (At-Tawbah-111).

Ayahs

سَابِقُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ مَغْفِرَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَجَنَّةٍ عَرْضُهَا كَعَرْضِ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ أُعِدَّتْ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٢١)
Take the lead towards forgiveness from your Lord and a paradise as vast as the heavens and the earth, prepared for those who have faith in Allah and His apostles. That is Allah’s grace, which He grants to whomever He wishes, and Allah is dispenser of a great grace. (21)
مَآ أَصَابَ مِن مُّصِيبَةٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مِّن قَبْلِ أَن نَّبْرَأَهَآ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ (٢٢)
No affliction visits the land or yourselves but it is in a Book before We bring it about—that is indeed easy for Allah— (22)
لِّكَيْلَا تَأْسَوْا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَاتَكُمْ وَلَا تَفْرَحُوا۟ بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ مُخْتَالٍ فَخُورٍ (٢٣)
so that you may not grieve for what escapes you, nor boast for what comes your way, and Allah does not like any swaggering braggart. (23)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ وَيَأْمُرُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ بِٱلْبُخْلِ ۗ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ (٢٤)
Such as are [themselves] stingy and bid [other] people to be stingy. And whoever refuses to comply [should know that] indeed Allah is the All-sufficient, the All-laudable. (24)

Commentary

A Great Spiritual Race

After explaining the instability of the world and its pleasures, and how people seek boastfulness and multiplication (Tafakhur and Takathur) against one another in the low-value assets of this world, the verses under discussion invite people to a magnificent spiritual race toward acquiring that which is lasting and worthy of every effort and endeavor, stating: "Race toward forgiveness from your Lord and a Garden whose width is like the width of the heavens and the earth, prepared for those who believe in Allah and His messengers" (سَابِقُوا إِلَىٰ مَغْفِرَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَجَنَّةٍ عَرْضُهَا كَعَرْضِ السَّمَاءِ وَالْأَرْضِ أُعِدَّتْ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِ).

In reality, the Lord's forgiveness is the key to Paradise—that very Paradise which encompasses the expanse of the heavens and the earth and is already prepared to welcome the believers. This is so that no one may say Paradise is a "deferred credit" and one should not set their heart on what is deferred; for even if it were deferred, it would be considered more "cash" than any immediate gain, as it is promised by a God Who is capable of all things, let alone the fact that it is fully "cash" and currently existing.

A similar meaning appears in verse 133 of Surah Al-Imran, with the difference being that here "Sabiqū" (from the root musabaqah, meaning to race) is used, while there "Sari'ū" (from the root musara'ah, meaning to hasten against one another) is used. These two are close in scope (considering the concept of the Mufa'alah form which depicts two parties striving to overcome each other). Another difference is that there it says "its width is the heavens and the earth," while here it says "like the width of the heavens and the earth" (كَعَرْضِ السَّمَاءِ وَالْأَرْضِ); with a little reflection, it becomes clear that both expressions convey the same reality.

Furthermore, there it says "prepared for the righteous (Muttaqin)," while here it says "for the believers." Since righteousness is the fruit of the tree of true faith, these two expressions are inseparable.

Thus, both verses pursue one reality with two different expressions. For this reason, what has been narrated from some—that the verse in Surah Al-Imran refers to the Paradise of the "Near Ones" (Muqarrabin) and the verse under discussion refers to the Paradise of the "Believers"—does not seem correct.

In any case, the term "width" ('ard) here is not in contrast to "length," as some commentators have suggested, leading them to search for the length of a Paradise whose width is like the heavens and the earth, thereby falling into difficulty! Rather, "width" in such usages means "expanse" or "vastness," similar to the expression "the breadth of the plain" in Persian, meaning the vastness of the desert.

The mention of "forgiveness" (maghfirah) before the glad tidings of Paradise, which appears in both verses, is a subtle allusion to the fact that until a person is purified from sin, they will not be worthy of entering Paradise and the proximity of the Lord.

It is also noteworthy that racing toward the Lord's forgiveness is achieved through its means, such as "repentance," "compensating for missed acts of worship," and fundamentally "obeying the Lord and avoiding sins."

If some traditions or commentaries emphasize certain obligatory or recommended acts—such as "racing to the first row of the congregational prayer," "the first row in Jihad," "the opening Takbir with the Imam," or "praying at the start of its time"—it is for the sake of providing examples or identifying clear instances, and it does not diminish the breadth of the verse's concept.

At the end of the verse, it adds: "That is the favor of Allah which He gives to whom He wills, and Allah is the possessor of great favor" (ذَٰلِكَ فَضْلُ اللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَنْ يَشَاءُ ۚ وَاللَّهُ ذُو الْفَضْلِ الْعَظِيمِ).

Certainly, such a vast Paradise with its magnificent blessings is not something a human can attain through these insignificant deeds; it is only the favor, mercy, and grace of Allah that has placed that great reward in exchange for this small amount, and nothing else is expected of Him. For rewards are not always measured by the scale of the deeds, but rather by the scale of the Generosity of the Rewarder. In any case, this expression clearly shows that the reward and recompense are not the "wages" of the deed, but rather a type of favor and mercy.

Then, to further emphasize the lack of attachment to the world, not rejoicing at its arrival, and not grieving over its departure, it adds: "No disaster strikes upon the earth or among yourselves except that it is in a Register (the Preserved Tablet) before We bring it into being—indeed that, for Allah, is easy" (مَا أَصَابَ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ إِلَّا فِي كِتَابٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ نَبْرَأَهَا ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى اللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ) [1]. Yes, the disasters that occur on earth, such as earthquakes, floods, storms, and various blights, as well as the disasters that occur within human souls, such as deaths and various painful incidents that afflict man, have all been predestined beforehand and are recorded in the Preserved Tablet.

However, it must be noted that the disasters referred to in this verse are only those that are entirely unavoidable and are not the product of human actions (in other words, the restriction here is relative).

The evidence for this is found in verse 30 of Surah Ash-Shura: "And whatever strikes you of disaster—it is for what your hands have earned; but He pardons much." Considering that verses explain one another, when these two verses are placed side by side, they show that the disasters afflicting man are of two kinds: disasters that are a punishment and expiation for his sins—and these are many; oppressions, injustices, betrayals, deviations, and ignorance are the source of many of our self-inflicted disasters.

But there is a portion of disasters in which we have no role, and they afflict an individual or a society as an inevitable and unavoidable matter. The accounting of these two is separate. Therefore, many Prophets, Saints (Awliya), and righteous people were afflicted with such disasters. These disasters have a precise philosophy which we have mentioned in discussions on theology, Divine Justice, and the issue of calamities and trials.

Thus, we read in a tradition: When Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام was brought into the assembly of Yazid in chains, Yazid turned to the Imam عليه السلام and recited the verse from Surah Ash-Shura: "And whatever strikes you of disaster—it is for what your hands have earned" (wishing to imply that the disasters of your family are the result of your own actions, and thus to taunt him). But the Imam immediately refuted his words and said: "Not so; this verse was not revealed concerning us. Rather, what was revealed concerning us is the other verse which says: 'No disaster strikes upon the earth or among yourselves except that it is in a Register before We bring it into being' (and it has a philosophy and wisdom)." [1] We had a more detailed discussion on this matter under verse 30 of Surah Ash-Shura (Volume 20, page 440). [2] The students of the school of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام also understood this meaning from the verse. As it is narrated, when Sa'id ibn Jubayr was brought before Hajjaj and he decided to kill him, a man among those present wept. Sa'id said: "Why do you weep?" He replied: "Because of this disaster that has befallen you." Sa'id said: "Do not weep; this was in the knowledge of God that it should happen. Have you not heard that Allah says: 'No disaster strikes upon the earth or among yourselves except that it is in a Register before We bring it into being'?" [1]

Of course, all events that occur in the world are recorded in the Preserved Tablet and the infinite knowledge of Allah. If here only disasters occurring on earth and in human souls are mentioned, it is because the subject of the discourse was this very meaning, and as we shall see, the conclusion in the following verse is drawn from this very subject.

Additionally, the phrase "indeed that, for Allah, is easy" points to the fact that recording and preserving all these events in the Preserved Tablet, despite their extraordinary multitude, is easy for Allah. The "Preserved Tablet" refers to the infinite knowledge of Allah, or the page of the world of creation and the system of cause and effect, which is also an instance of Allah's active knowledge (reflect deeply).

Now let us see what the philosophy is behind predestining these disasters in the Preserved Tablet and then stating this reality in the Quran. The next verse pulls back the curtain on this important secret, saying: "In order that you not despair over what has eluded you and not exult [in pride] over what He has given you" (لِكَيْلَا تَأْسَوْا عَلَىٰ مَا فَاتَكُمْ وَلَا تَفْرَحُوا بِمَا آتَاكُمْ).

These two short sentences actually solve one of the complex issues of the philosophy of creation. For man is always faced with problems, hardships, and unpleasant events in the universe, and often asks himself: "While Allah is Kind, Generous, and Merciful, what are these painful events for?!" The Quran says: "The goal was that you should not be attached to or enslaved by the glitter of this world."

The intention was that you should not lose sight of the nature of this passage and bridge called "the world," nor your own position in this world; that you should not become infatuated and enamored with it, nor consider it eternal, for this extreme attachment is the greatest enemy of your happiness. It makes you heedless of the remembrance of Allah and holds you back from the path of perfection.

These disasters are a wake-up call for the heedless, a whip upon sleeping souls, a symbol of the instability of the world, and an allusion to the shortness of this life's span. The truth is that the deceptive manifestations of this "Abode of Delusion" (Dar al-Ghurur) quickly attract and preoccupy man, making him forget the Truth. Suddenly he wakes up to find the caravan has gone, he was asleep, and the desert lies ahead.

These events, which have always been in life and always will be—and even the great advances of science have not been and will not be able to prevent painful events such as earthquakes, storms, floods, and diseases—are a lesson in the "unkindness of the scandalous times" and cry out to man:

"This plain is the sleeping place of martyrs; Count the time of viewing as an opportunity."

This does not mean that man should turn his back on Divine blessings in this world or not benefit from them; the important thing is that he should not become their slave, nor consider them his ultimate goal and primary gain.

It is noteworthy that regarding what is lost, He uses "Fatakum" (eluded you), but regarding the blessings and favors obtained, He attributes them to God and says "Bima Atakum" (what He has given you). This is because passing away and annihilation are inherent in the essence of things, while existence is what originates from the grace of Allah.

Yes, these disasters break boastfulness and pride; therefore, at the end of the verse, it adds: "And Allah does not love any self-deluded boaster" (وَاللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ مُخْتَالٍ فَخُورٍ).

"Mukhtal" is derived from the root khayal (imagination) and means "arrogant," because arrogance arises from the imagination of virtue and the delusion of superiority over others. "Fakhur" is an intensive form from the root fakhr, meaning one who boasts excessively to others.

Only those who become intoxicated with luxury and blessings fall into these states, but the existence of blights and disasters for those capable of awakening and guidance eliminates this intoxication and its effects.

People of faith, mindful of the above principle, consider themselves His trustees when they receive a blessing from Allah. They are neither grieved by its departure nor intoxicated and arrogant by its possession. In reality, they see themselves like the officials of the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) who receive large amounts of wealth one day and pay out thousands upon thousands the next; they are neither thrilled by receiving it nor grieved by paying it out.

And what a beautiful expression Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام has regarding this verse when he says: "Asceticism (Zuhd) is entirely between two sentences of the Quran: Allah the Almighty said: 'In order that you not despair over what has eluded you and not exult over what He has given you.' Therefore, whoever does not regret the past and is not [excessively] attached to what is in his hand has grasped asceticism by both its ends!" [1]

Another point is that awareness of this principle—that setbacks have been intertwined with human life from the beginning and have been predestined according to a wise Sunnah, and that the world constantly has ups and downs—makes a person brave in enduring hardships and patient and resilient in the face of severe events. It gives him peace of mind and prevents restlessness and anguish.

But we emphasize again: this applies only to unavoidable disasters; otherwise, disasters and failures that are solely the result of one's own sins and negligence are outside this discussion, and the way to confront them is through correct positioning in life's plans.

We end this discussion with a story mentioned by some commentators: "Qutaybah ibn Sa'id" [2] says: I entered one of the Arab tribes and saw a desert full of camels, all of which had died, beyond counting. There was an old woman there. I asked: "To whom did these camels belong?" She said: "To that old man you see sitting on that hill spinning wool." I went to him and said: "Were all these yours?!" He said: "They were in my name!" I said: "What happened that they ended up like this?"

In response (without mentioning the blight or the cause of their death), he said: "The One who gave took back." I said: "Are you not upset? Have you said anything regarding this?" He said: "Yes, I have said these two verses:

'No, by the One whose servant I am among His creatures— Man in this age is a target for calamity and trials. It would not have pleased me had I been tested [with their survival] in their resting places— And that which occurred by the decree of Allah had not been!'"

(I am satisfied only with His pleasure, and whatever He has willed, I prefer that same thing.) [1]

The last verse under discussion is an explanation and commentary on what appeared in the previous verse and, in reality, identifies the "self-deluded boaster" (Mukhtal Fakhur). It says: "[Those] who are stingy and enjoin upon people stinginess" (الَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ وَيَأْمُرُونَ النَّاسَ بِالْبُخْلِ) [2]. Yes, the consequence of intense attachment to the blessings of the world is arrogance and pride, and the consequence of arrogance and pride is being stingy and inviting others to stinginess. He is stingy because he considers this wealth the capital of his pride and arrogance and never wants to lose it. And he invites others to stinginess because, firstly, if others are generous, he will be disgraced, and secondly, because he loves stinginess, he promotes that which he adores!

To ensure it is not imagined that Allah's insistence and emphasis on the issue of spending (Infaq) and abandoning stinginess—or even the expression in previous verses about "Allah taking a loan from His servants," all of which are to encourage them to spend—stems from a need of His Pure Essence, the end of the verse adds: "And whoever turns away—then indeed, Allah is the Free of need, the Praiseworthy" (وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ الْحَمِيدُ).

Everyone is in need of Him, and He is free of need from everyone, for the treasuries and primary sources of everything are with Him. And since He encompasses all attributes of perfection, He is worthy of every praise and commendation.

Although "stinginess" (bukhl) in the above verse mostly refers to stinginess in financial spending, this term has a broad concept that also includes stinginess in knowledge, fulfilling rights, and the like.

Ayah

لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلَنَا بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَأَنزَلْنَا مَعَهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ لِيَقُومَ ٱلنَّاسُ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ وَأَنزَلْنَا ٱلْحَدِيدَ فِيهِ بَأْسٌ شَدِيدٌ وَمَنَـٰفِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَنصُرُهُۥ وَرُسُلَهُۥ بِٱلْغَيْبِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَوِىٌّ عَزِيزٌ (٢٥)
Certainly We sent Our apostles with manifest proofs, and We sent down with them the Book and the Balance, so that mankind may maintain justice; and We sent down iron, in which there is great might and uses for mankind, and so that Allah may know those who help Him and His apostles [with faith] in the Unseen. Indeed Allah is all-strong, all-mighty. (25)

Commentary

The Primary Goal of the Mission of the Prophets

Since racing toward the mercy, forgiveness, and Paradise of the Lord—which was mentioned in the previous verses—requires the leadership of "Divine Leaders," the verse under discussion, which is among the most profound verses of the Quran, points to this reality. It precisely explains the purpose of sending the Prophets and their program, stating: "We have already sent Our messengers with clear proofs" (لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا رُسُلَنا بِالْبَيِّناتِ‌).

"And sent down with them the Scripture and the balance" (وَ أَنْزَلْنا مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَ الْمِيزانَ‌).

"That the people may maintain [their affairs] in justice" (لِيَقُومَ النَّاسُ بِالْقِسْطِ).

"Bayanat" (clear proofs) has a broad meaning that includes "miracles" and "rational arguments" with which the Prophets and Divine Messengers were equipped.

The "Scripture" refers to the Heavenly Books; and since the spirit and reality of all of them are one, the term "Scripture" is used in the singular form, even though its content becomes more complete with the passage of time and the evolution of humanity.

As for the "Balance" (Mizan), it means "a means of weighing and measuring." Its physical manifestation is the scales used to weigh goods, but certainly, what is meant here is its spiritual manifestation—that is, something by which all human actions can be measured. This refers to the Divine rulings and laws, or His religion in general, which serves as the criteria for measuring good and evil, values and anti-values.

Thus, the Prophets were equipped with three tools: clear proofs, Heavenly Books, and the criteria for distinguishing truth from falsehood and good from evil. There is no obstacle to, for example, the Magnificent Quran being simultaneously a "proof" (miracle), a Heavenly Book, and an explainer of rulings and laws—meaning three dimensions within one content.

In any case, the goal of sending these great men with such complete equipment is the implementation of "equity and justice."

In fact, this verse points to one of the multiple goals of sending the Prophets, for we know that the Prophets pursued several objectives.

One is education and purification, as stated in verse 2 of Surah Al-Jumu'ah: هُوَ الَّذِي بَعَثَ فِي الْأُمِّيِّينَ رَسُولًا مِنْهُمْ يَتْلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ آياتِهِ وَ يُزَكِّيهِمْ وَ يُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَ الْحِكْمَةَ:

"It is He who has sent among the unlettered a Messenger from themselves reciting to them His verses and purifying them and teaching them the Book and wisdom."

Another goal is breaking the shackles and chains of captivity, as we read in verse 157 of Surah Al-A'raf: وَ يَضَعُ عَنْهُمْ إِصْرَهُمْ وَ الْأَغْلالَ الَّتِي كانَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ‌:

"The Prophet of Islam removes from them their heavy burdens and the shackles which were upon them."

Another goal is the perfection of moral values, as stated in the famous Hadith: "I was only sent to perfect noble character" (بعثت لاتمم مكارم الاخلاق) [1]. And finally, another goal is the "establishment of equity," which is mentioned in the verse under discussion. Thus, the goals of the Prophets' mission can be summarized into "cultural," "moral," "political," and "social" objectives.

It goes without saying that "Messengers" in the verse under discussion, by the context of "sending down the Books," refers to the "Arch-Prophets" (Ulul-'Azm) and those like them.

Another interesting point in the phrase "that the people may maintain [their affairs] in justice" (لِيَقُومَ النَّاسُ بِالْقِسْطِ) is that it speaks of the "spontaneous action of the people." It does not say the goal was for the Prophets to force people to establish equity; rather, it says the goal was for the people themselves to be the executors of equity and justice! Indeed, the important thing is for people to be so developed that they themselves become the executors of justice and tread this path with their own feet.

However, since in any human society—no matter how high the level of morality, belief, and piety—there will still be individuals who rise in rebellion and stubbornness and prevent the implementation of equity and justice, the verse continues:

"And We sent down iron, wherein is great military might and benefits for the people!" (وَ أَنْزَلْنَا الْحَدِيدَ فِيهِ بَأْسٌ شَدِيدٌ وَ مَنافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ‌).

Yes, the three-fold equipment of the Divine Prophets for the implementation of justice can only reach its ultimate goal when it enjoys the executive guarantee of iron and its "great might."

Although some have imagined that the expression "We sent down" (Anzalna) indicates that "iron" came to Earth from other celestial bodies, the truth is that the expression "sending down" in such cases refers to blessings given from a higher station to lower stations. Since the treasuries of all things are with Allah and He is the one who created iron for its various benefits, the term "sending down" is used. Therefore, in a Hadith from Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام, it is stated regarding the interpretation of this phrase: "Its sending down is His creation of it" (انزاله ذلك خلقه اياه) [1], just as we read in verse 6 of Surah Az-Zumar regarding livestock:

وَ أَنْزَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَنْعامِ ثَمانِيَةَ أَزْواجٍ‌: "And He sent down for you eight pairs of livestock."

Some have also interpreted "Anzalna" from the root "Nuzul" (like the provision for a camel) meaning something prepared for the hospitality of a guest, but the first meaning is the apparent one.

"Bas" (might) linguistically means intensity, strength, and power, and combat and war are also called "Bas." Therefore, some commentators have interpreted it as military equipment, both defensive and offensive. In a narration from Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of this verse, he said: "It means weapons and other things" (يعنى السلاح و غير ذلك) [2]. It is clear that this is by way of providing an example.

[1] and [2]: Tafsir "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 250 (Hadith 100 and 101).

"Benefits" refers to every kind of use that humans derive from iron. We know that the importance of iron in human life is so great that its discovery marked a new era in human history known as the Iron Age, because with this discovery, the face of human life was transformed in all fields. This itself illustrates the dimensions of the word "benefits" in the above verse.

In the Magnificent Quran, this meaning is pointed out in various verses: in one place, it says when Dhul-Qarnayn decided to build his great dam, he said:

آتُونِي زُبَرَ الْحَدِيدِ: "Bring me sheets of iron" (Al-Kahf: 96).

And when Allah favored David with His grace, He softened iron for him so he could make coats of mail to reduce the dangers of wars and enemy attacks: وَ أَلَنَّا لَهُ الْحَدِيدَ أَنِ اعْمَلْ سابِغاتٍ (Saba: 10-11).

Then, it points to another goal of sending the Prophets, the revelation of Heavenly Books, and the creation of means such as iron, stating: "That Allah may make evident those who support Him and His messengers unseen" (وَ لِيَعْلَمَ اللَّهُ مَنْ يَنْصُرُهُ وَ رُسُلَهُ بِالْغَيْبِ‌).

The meaning of Allah's "knowledge" here is the objective realization of His knowledge—that is, so it becomes manifest who rises to support Allah and His school of thought and establishes equity, and who turns away from this great duty. In fact, the concept of this verse is similar to what appears in verse 179 of Surah Al-Imran: ما كانَ اللَّهُ لِيَذَرَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ عَلى‌ ما أَنْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ حَتَّى يَمِيزَ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ‌:

"Allah would not leave the believers in that [state] you are in [now] until He separates the evil from the good." Thus, the issue of testing and examining humans, and the separation of ranks and purification, has been another great goal of this program.

The expression "supporting Allah" certainly means supporting His religion, His creed, and His representatives, and spreading the religion of truth, equity, and justice; otherwise, Allah has no need for anyone's help, and everyone is in need of Him. Therefore, to prove this very point, He ends the verse with the phrase: "Indeed, Allah is Powerful and Exalted in Might" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ قَوِيٌّ عَزِيزٌ).

It is possible for Him to overturn the entire world with a single gesture, destroy all enemies, and make His friends victorious, but the primary goal—which is the education and perfection of humans—is not achieved through that path. Therefore, He has invited them to support the religion of truth.

Key Points

1- The Domain of "Logic" and "Force"!

The above verse provides a vivid depiction of Islam's face in the fields of education, the spread of justice, and the implementation of equity in human society.

First, it seeks help from "clear proofs," "Heavenly Books," and the "criteria for measuring values and explaining rulings and laws"—meaning it places the foundation on an intellectual and cultural revolution and seeks aid from reason and logic.

But if these are not effective and the situation reaches a deadlock—meaning powerful tyrants emerge who neither submit to clear proofs nor value the Book and the Balance—then comes the turn for "iron" in which there is "great might." With weapons, the heads of these stubborn ones are struck until they submit to equity and justice, and of course, the help of the faithful people is sought in this path.

The Hadith narrated from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله stating: "I was sent with the sword before the Hour, until Allah alone is worshipped without partner, and my provision was placed under the shadow of my spear" (بعثت بالسيف بين يدى الساعة...) [1] refers to this very point—meaning I am commanded to use weapons against this group, not as the primary basis of the work, as explicitly stated in the above verse.

In another Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "All good is in the sword, under the sword, and in the shadow of the sword" (الخير كله فى السيف...) [2]. And in a Hadith from Imam Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام, we read: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has ordained Jihad and magnified it, and made it His victory and His helper; by Allah, neither the world nor religion is rectified except by it" (ان اللَّه عز و جل فرض الجهاد...) [3]. We conclude this discussion with another Hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله: "Nothing maintains the people except the sword, and swords are the keys to Paradise and the Fire" (لا يقيم الناس الا السيف...) [4]. Therefore, Divine leaders have Heavenly Books in one hand and the sword in the other; first, they invite people to truth and justice with logic, and whenever tyrants do not submit to logic, they resort to force against them.

2- Major Life Needs Depend on Iron

Some commentators have an analysis of the above verse, the summary of which is as follows:

[1]: Tafsir al-Maraghi, Vol. 27, p. 183. [2] and [3]: Furu' al-Kafi, Vol. 5, p. 8, Hadith 15 and 11. [4]: Furu' al-Kafi, Vol. 5, p. 2, Hadith 1.

The principles of human life are four things: agriculture, weaving (industry), housing, and governance. This is because humans need food, clothing, and shelter, and since man is a social being, he cannot solve life's problems alone; or in other words, the interests of the community are secured by the community.

And since every community is inevitably not free from conflict and clash of interests, it therefore needs a government to implement justice among them. Surprisingly, these four principles all require iron; if this tool did not exist, life would be very difficult for humans. Since the need for it is great, Allah created it in abundance and made it easily accessible (it is true that other metals each play a role in human life, but the primary role belongs to iron). From here, the concept of "wherein is great military might and benefits for the people" becomes clear [1].

[1]: Adapted from Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 29, p. 242.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا وَإِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَجَعَلْنَا فِى ذُرِّيَّتِهِمَا ٱلنُّبُوَّةَ وَٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ۖ فَمِنْهُم مُّهْتَدٍ ۖ وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ (٢٦)
Certainly We sent Noah and Abraham and We ordained among their descendants prophethood and the Book. Some of them are [rightly] guided, and many of them are transgressors. (26)
ثُمَّ قَفَّيْنَا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِم بِرُسُلِنَا وَقَفَّيْنَا بِعِيسَى ٱبْنِ مَرْيَمَ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلْإِنجِيلَ وَجَعَلْنَا فِى قُلُوبِ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوهُ رَأْفَةً وَرَحْمَةً وَرَهْبَانِيَّةً ٱبْتَدَعُوهَا مَا كَتَبْنَـٰهَا عَلَيْهِمْ إِلَّا ٱبْتِغَآءَ رِضْوَٰنِ ٱللَّهِ فَمَا رَعَوْهَا حَقَّ رِعَايَتِهَا ۖ فَـَٔاتَيْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ أَجْرَهُمْ ۖ وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ (٢٧)
Then We followed them up with Our apostles and We followed [them] with Jesus son of Mary, and We gave him the Evangel, and We put kindness and mercy into the hearts of those who followed him. But as for monasticism, they innovated it—We had not prescribed it for them—only seeking Allah’s pleasure. Yet they did not observe it with due observance. So We gave to the faithful among them their [due] reward, but many of them are transgressors. (27)

Commentary

We Sent the Prophets One After Another

As we know, the method of the Quran is that after stating a series of general principles of its teachings, it refers to the fate of past nations to serve as a living witness for them. Here too, after mentioning the previous issues regarding the sending of messengers accompanied by clear proofs, the Book, and the Balance, as well as the necessity of people racing with one another to attain the Lord's forgiveness and eternal happiness, it names some of the past nations and Prophets and personifies these general principles in their lives.

It begins first with "Noah" and "Abraham," who were the Shaykh of the Prophets and the heads of the lineage of the messengers of Truth, saying: "And We sent Noah and Abraham, and placed in their offspring prophethood and the Scripture" (وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا وَإِبْرَاهِيمَ وَجَعَلْنَا فِي ذُرِّيَّتِهِمَا النُّبُوَّةَ وَالْكِتَابَ).

However, not everyone benefited from this great heritage and the immense blessings of Allah: "Some of them were guided, but many of them are defiant and disobedient" (فَمِنْهُمْ مُهْتَدٍ وَكَثِيرٌ مِنْهُمْ فَاسِقُونَ).

Indeed, prophethood accompanied by Divine Law and religion began with Noah (peace be upon him), and after him, Abraham (peace be upon him), another Arch-Prophet (Ulul-'Azm), continued this line. It persisted through the ages and centuries among their children and descendants, yet a minority always benefited from this light of guidance, while the majority pursued the path of error!

Then, making a brief reference to the succession of other Prophets, and the last of them before the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), it adds: "Then We followed them up with Our messengers" (ثُمَّ قَفَّيْنَا عَلَىٰ آثَارِهِمْ بِرُسُلِنَا). They rose one after another and placed the lamp of guidance in the path of the people until it was the turn of Prophet Jesus (peace be upon him).

"And We followed them with Jesus, son of Mary" (وَقَفَّيْنَا بِعِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ).

"Qaffayna" is derived from the root "qafa," meaning "back" or "behind." The rhyme in poetry is called "qafiyah" because the final parts of the verses are similar to one another and follow one after another. In the above sentence, the meaning is that the Prophets entered the stage of existence one after another with a single, consistent tone and harmonious goals, confirming and completing each other's teachings. In truth, this expression is a beautiful reference to the "Unity of Prophethood."

Then, referring to the Heavenly Book of Jesus (peace be upon him), it adds: "And We gave him the Gospel" (وَآتَيْنَاهُ الْإِنْجِيلَ).

Afterward, it speaks of the characteristics of his followers, saying: "And We placed in the hearts of those who followed him compassion and mercy" (وَجَعَلْنَا فِي قُلُوبِ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُ رَأْفَةً وَرَحْمَةً). Some commentators consider these two terms (ra'fah and rahmah) to have the same meaning, but a group distinguishes between them: "ra'fah" means affection in the context of repelling harm, while "rahmah" means affection for the sake of attracting benefit. Therefore, ra'fah is often mentioned before rahmah, because one must first repel harm and then think of attracting benefit. For this same reason, in the verse regarding the punishment of adulterers, it says:

وَلَا تَأْخُذْكُمْ بِهِمَا رَأْفَةٌ فِي دِينِ اللَّهِ "And let not compassion for them withhold you from [executing] the religion of Allah [i.e., His law]..." (An-Nur: 2).

The matter of the compassion and mercy of the true followers of Jesus is not something mentioned only in this verse; rather, in verse 82 of Al-Ma'idah, we also read:

وَلَتَجِدَنَّ أَقْرَبَهُمْ مَوَدَّةً لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّا نَصَارَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ مِنْهُمْ قِسِّيسِينَ وَرُهْبَانًا وَأَنَّهُمْ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ "And you will find the nearest of them in affection to the believers those who say, 'We are Christians.' That is because among them are priests and monks and because they are not arrogant."

Although this verse primarily refers to the Christians of Abyssinia and the person of "Najashi" (the Negus) who gave refuge to the Muslims and treated them with special kindness and affection, it generally points to the compassion, mercy, and positive emotions of true Christians—not those bloodthirsty wolves and human-like demons who in our era have taken the name of Christianity upon themselves and have dragged the world into blood, fire, plunder, and looting!

Then it adds: "And monasticism, which they innovated; We did not prescribe it for them except [that they did it] seeking the approval of Allah. But they did not observe it with due observance. So We gave the ones who believed among them their reward, but many of them are defiant and disobedient" (وَرَهْبَانِيَّةً ابْتَدَعُوهَا مَا كَتَبْنَاهَا عَلَيْهِمْ إِلَّا ابْتِغَاءَ رِضْوَانِ اللَّهِ فَمَا رَعَوْهَا حَقَّ رِعَايَتِهَا فَآتَيْنَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْهُمْ أَجْرَهُمْ وَكَثِيرٌ مِنْهُمْ فَاسِقُونَ) [1].

[1]: There is much discussion among commentators regarding the syntax and meaning of this verse. Some consider it conjoined to "compassion and mercy" and assume the word "love" is implied before "monasticism," because monasticism is not something that resides in the heart, but rather the love and inclination for it is in the heart. A group considers it the object of a hidden verb explained by "ib tada'uha" (they innovated it), as if it were: "They innovated monasticism, they innovated it." Regarding "except seeking the approval of Allah," there are two views: first, that it is a "disjointed exception" (istithna' munqati'), meaning: "but they innovated it seeking the approval of Allah." Second, that it is a "connected exception" (istithna' muttasil), meaning: "We had prescribed for them a type of monasticism whose goal was seeking Allah's pleasure, but they distorted it into another form contrary to the Truth's pleasure." It seems the first interpretation is more suitable in both cases (reflect on this).

In this way, they not only failed to observe the monotheistic religion of Jesus and tainted it with various forms of polytheism, but they also failed to observe the rights of the monasticism they themselves had innovated. In the name of asceticism and monasticism, they spread traps in the path of Allah's creation, turned monasteries into centers of various corruptions, and created abnormalities in the religion of Jesus (peace be upon him).

According to this interpretation, "monasticism" was not part of the religion of Jesus; rather, his followers innovated it after him. In the beginning, it had a moderate and mild face, but later it drifted into deviation and resulted in many corruptions.

However, according to another interpretation, there was a type of monasticism and asceticism in the religion of Jesus; what the followers of Jesus innovated after him was another type of monasticism that Allah had never prescribed for them [1].

[1]: The first interpretation follows the "disjointed exception," and the second follows the "connected exception" (reflect on this). It is also noteworthy that if monasticism is conjoined to "compassion and mercy," as we have chosen in the text, the meaning of "placing it in their hearts" is their heart's inclination toward this matter, while "We did not prescribe it" means that the issue of monasticism was not a Divine decree in the religion of Jesus, even though Allah had cast an inclination for it into their hearts. Therefore, it does not contradict the phrase "they innovated it."

However, the first interpretation is more famous and more suitable in some respects.

In any case, from the verse as a whole, it is understood that monasticism was not in the religion of Jesus and his followers innovated it after him, but in the beginning, it was considered a type of asceticism and a good innovation, like many of the good customs and traditions currently common among people, which no one relies upon as a specific legislation or religious command. But this tradition later drifted into deviation and became tainted with matters contrary to the Divine command and even ugly sins. The Quran's expression "they did not observe it with due observance" is proof that if its rights had been fulfilled, it would have been a good tradition. The expression in verse 82 of Al-Ma'idah, which mentions the "monks" and "scholars" of the true Christians with a favorable and positive view, is also a witness to this claim (reflect on this).

And whenever we consider the word "monasticism" alongside "compassion and mercy," another witness for this claim is found, as it becomes aligned with the compassion and mercy that Allah placed in their hearts as a commendable quality.

In short, if a good tradition becomes common among people whose general principles (such as the command for asceticism) are in the religion of Truth, and people do not attribute that tradition specifically to a Divine command but rather consider it an instance of general commands and fulfill its rights, it is not a bad thing. Misfortune begins when excesses and deficiencies arise and turn it into an evil tradition.

For example, currently among us, ceremonies for mourning, celebrations, and the death anniversaries of the great leaders of religion, as well as the commemoration of martyrs and lost loved ones on the day of their martyrdom or their seventh, fortieth, and annual anniversaries, are common. These are instances of the general commands of Islam regarding the honoring of Divine symbols and the commemoration of religious leaders, martyrs, and the general body of Muslims. They are also instances of the general principle of "mourning for the martyrs of Karbala" and the like, without the details and specifics of these ceremonies being considered a specific religious command; rather, they are performed merely as an instance of that general principle.

Whenever these ceremonies do not exceed the limits of the Sharia and do not become tainted with sin or superstition, they are certainly an instance of "seeking the approval of Allah" and an instance of a good tradition (sunnah hasanah). Otherwise, they would be an ominous innovation (bid'ah) and an evil tradition.

It seems that "monasticism" (rahbaniyyah), derived from the root "rahbah" meaning fear of Allah, was initially an instance of asceticism and indifference toward the world, but later it was drawn into extensive distortions. If we see that Islam strongly combats this monasticism, it is because of this final face of it, as we will explain in more detail, Allah willing, in the Key Points.

Key Points

1- Islam and Monasticism

As we said, "monasticism" (rahbaniyyah) is from the root "rahbah," meaning fear and dread, which here refers to the fear and dread of Allah. According to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, it is a fear mixed with caution and anxiety, and "tarahhub" means "ta'abbud" (devotion) and worship, and "rahbaniyyah" means intensity of devotion.

However one interprets the above verse, it shows that a type of desirable monasticism existed among the Christians, even though no such mandatory command was given to them in the religion of Jesus. But the followers of Jesus took that monasticism beyond its boundaries and led it into deviation and distortion.

For this reason, Islam has strongly condemned it, and the famous hadith: "There is no monasticism in Islam" (la rahbaniyyata fil-Islam) is found in many Islamic sources [1]. Among the ugly innovations of the Christians in the field of monasticism was the "prohibition of marriage" for men and women who renounced the world, and another was "social isolation," turning one's back on human duties in society, and choosing remote monasteries and cloisters for worship and living in an environment far from society. Subsequently, many corruptions arose in the monasteries and living centers of the monks, some of which we will mention later, Allah willing, to complete this discussion.

It is true that the men and women who renounced the world (monks and nuns) also performed positive services, including nursing those with incurable and dangerous diseases like lepers, and proselytizing in very remote areas and among primitive tribes, and the like, as well as study and research programs. But these matters were insignificant and of little importance compared to the overall program, and as a whole, its corruptions far outweighed its benefits.

Fundamentally, the human being is a creature made for living in society, and his spiritual and material perfection lies in having a collective life. Therefore, none of the Heavenly religions deny this meaning for man; rather, they strengthen its foundations.

Allah created the "sexual instinct" in man to preserve the species; anything that absolutely denies it is certainly false.

Islamic asceticism (zuhd), which means simplicity of life, the elimination of luxuries, and not being enslaved by wealth and status, has no connection to the issue of monasticism. This is because monasticism means separation and alienation from society, while asceticism means freedom and detachment for the sake of living more socially.

In a famous hadith, we read that "Uthman ibn Maz'un" had lost his son and became very grieved, to the point that he turned his house into a mosque and engaged in worship (leaving every task except worship). This news reached the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family). He summoned him and said:

يَا عُثْمَانُ! إِنَّ اللَّهَ تَبَارَكَ وَتَعَالَى لَمْ يَكْتُبْ عَلَيْنَا الرَّهْبَانِيَّةَ، إِنَّمَا رَهْبَانِيَّةُ أُمَّتِي الْجِهَادُ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ! "O Uthman! Allah the Almighty has not prescribed monasticism for my nation; the monasticism of my nation is Jihad in the way of Allah!" [1] This points to the fact that if you want to turn your back on material life, do not do it in a negative way through social isolation, but seek it in a positive path, namely Jihad in the way of Allah.

Then the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) gave him a detailed explanation regarding the virtue of congregational prayer, which is a confirmation of the rejection of monasticism and isolation.

In another hadith from Imam Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him), we read that his brother "Ali ibn Ja'far" asked him:

الرَّجُلُ الْمُسْلِمُ هَلْ يَصْلُحُ أَنْ يَسِيحَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَوْ يَتَرَهَّبَ فِي بَيْتٍ لَا يَخْرُجُ مِنْهُ؟ قَالَ (ع) لَا: "Is it proper for a Muslim man to wander (yasih) through the land or to adopt monasticism in a house from which he does not emerge? The Imam (peace be upon him) replied: No." [1] The explanation is that the "wandering" (siyahat) forbidden in this narration was something equivalent to monasticism—a kind of mobile monasticism. This meant that some individuals, without providing a home or life for themselves or having a trade or business, would constantly go from one place to another as travelers without provisions, living by taking help from people and begging, and they considered it a type of asceticism and renunciation of the world. But Islam has rejected both fixed monasticism and mobile monasticism. Yes, according to Islamic teachings, the important thing is for a person to be detached and ascetic in the heart of society, not in isolation and alienation from society!

[1]: In Majma' al-Bahrayn, under the root rahab, this hadith is mentioned, and it is also cited in Ibn al-Athir's An-Nihayah. [1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 70, p. 114 (Chapter on the Prohibition of Monasticism, Hadith 1). [1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 70, p. 119, Hadith 10.

2- The Historical Origin of Monasticism

Existing histories of Christianity show that monasticism in its current form did not exist in the first centuries of Christianity. Its emergence is considered to be after the third century AD, during the rise of a Roman emperor named "Decius" and his severe struggle against the followers of Jesus (peace be upon him). Upon their defeat by this (bloodthirsty) emperor, they fled to the mountains and deserts [2]. In Islamic narrations, this same meaning is reported more precisely from the noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), who one day said to "Ibn Mas'ud": "Do you know where monasticism came from?" He replied: "Allah and His Messenger know best." He said:

After Jesus (peace be upon him), a group of tyrants appeared, and the believers fought them three times and were defeated. Therefore, they fled to the deserts and engaged in worship in mountain caves, waiting for the appearance of the promised Prophet of Jesus (Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him and his family). Some of them remained firm in their religion, while others took the path of disbelief.

Then he added: "Do you know what the monasticism of my nation is?" He replied: "Allah and His Messenger know best." He said: "Migration (Hijrah), Jihad, Prayer, Fasting, Hajj, and Umrah."

الْهِجْرَةُ، وَالْجِهَادُ، وَالصَّلَاةُ، وَالصَّوْمُ، وَالْحَجُّ، وَالْعُمْرَةُ [1]

The famous Christian historian Will Durant, in his well-known history, Vol. 13, provides a detailed discussion regarding monks. He believes that the joining of "nuns" (women who renounced the world) to "monks" began in the fourth century AD and the practice of monasticism grew day by day until it reached its peak in the tenth century AD [2]. Undoubtedly, this social phenomenon, like any other phenomenon, has psychological roots in addition to historical ones. Among these, one can point to the fact that fundamentally, the psychological reaction of different individuals and nations to defeats and failures is completely different. Some develop a tendency toward isolation and introversion and distance themselves completely from society and social activities, while another group learns the lesson of steadfastness from defeat and gains more solidity and resistance. The first group turns to monasticism or something similar, while the second group, conversely, becomes more social.

[2]: Encyclopaedia of the Twentieth Century, under the root rahab. [1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 243 (with slight abridgment). In Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur, another similar hadith is narrated (Vol. 6, p. 177). [2]: Will Durant's History, Vol. 13, p. 443.

3- Moral and Social Corruptions Resulting from Monasticism

Deviation from the laws of creation always brings about negative reactions. Therefore, it is not surprising that when a person distances himself from the social life that is in his nature and instinct, he suffers severe negative consequences. Thus, monasticism, by virtue of being contrary to the principles of human nature and instinct, brings about many corruptions, including:

1- Monasticism conflicts with the spirit of man being "civil by nature" and drags human societies into decline and regression.

2- Monasticism is not only not a cause for the perfection of the soul and the refinement of spirit and morals, but it leads to moral deviations, laziness, pessimism, pride, vanity, self-superiority, and the like. Even assuming a person could reach moral virtue in isolation, it is not considered a virtue. Virtue is when a person can rid himself of moral pollutions in the heart of society.

3- Renouncing marriage, which is one of the principles of monasticism, not only creates no perfection but causes the emergence of complexes and psychological illnesses.

In the Encyclopaedia of the Twentieth Century, we read: "Some monks considered attention to the female gender to be a satanic act to such an extent that they were not willing to bring a female animal into the house! Lest its satanic spirit damage their spirituality"!! Yet, despite this, history remembers many atrocities from the monasteries, to the point that according to Will Durant, Pope Innocent III described one of the monasteries as a brothel! [1]

And some of them became centers for the gathering of gluttons, world-seekers, and pleasure-hunters, to the point that the best wines were found in monasteries.

Of course, according to the testimony of history, Prophet Jesus never married, but this was never due to his opposition to this matter; rather, the short life of Jesus, added to his constant engagement in missionary travels to different parts of the world, did not allow him this matter.

The discussion about monasticism deserves an independent book; if we were to address it, we would step outside the exegetical discussion.

We end this discussion with a hadith from Ali (peace be upon him): In a hadith regarding the verse:

قُلْ هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِالْأَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمَالًا الَّذِينَ ضَلَّ سَعْيُهُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَهُمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعًا "Say, [O Muhammad], 'Shall we [believers] inform you of the greatest losers as to [their] deeds? [They are] those whose effort is lost in worldly life, while they think that they are doing well in work'" (Al-Kahf: 103-104).

Ali (peace be upon him) said in its interpretation: "They are the monks who imprisoned themselves in the heights" (هُمُ الرُّهْبَانُ الَّذِينَ حَبَسُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ فِي السَّوَارِي): "One of its clear instances is the monks who kept themselves imprisoned in the heights of mountains and deserts and thought they were doing a good deed" [2].

4- "Gospel" or "Gospels"

"Gospel" (Injil) is originally a Greek word meaning "good news" or "new teaching," and it is the name of the book revealed to Prophet Jesus.

[1]: Will Durant, Vol. 13, p. 443. [2]: Kanz al-Ummal, Vol. 2, Hadith 4496.

This word is used 12 times in the Holy Quran with this same meaning, and everywhere it is in the singular form. However, it is noteworthy that what is known as the Gospel today consists of many books referred to as "Gospels" (Anajil), and the famous ones among them are the four Gospels of "Luke," "Mark," "Matthew," and "John." Christians believe that these four Gospels were written by these four companions of Jesus (peace be upon him) or their disciples, and the date of their composition ranges from 38 years after Prophet Jesus to about a century after him. Therefore, the original book of Jesus as an independent Heavenly book has been consigned to oblivion, and only parts of it that were in the memory of these four individuals have been written in these four Gospels, mixed with their own thoughts.

We had a more detailed discussion in this regard under verse 3 of Al-Imran [1].

[1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 2, p. 311.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَءَامِنُوا۟ بِرَسُولِهِۦ يُؤْتِكُمْ كِفْلَيْنِ مِن رَّحْمَتِهِۦ وَيَجْعَل لَّكُمْ نُورًا تَمْشُونَ بِهِۦ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٢٨)
O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and have faith in His Apostle. He will grant you a double share of His mercy and a light to walk by, and He will forgive you, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful; (28)
لِّئَلَّا يَعْلَمَ أَهْلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أَلَّا يَقْدِرُونَ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ مِّن فَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ ۙ وَأَنَّ ٱلْفَضْلَ بِيَدِ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٢٩)
so that the People of the Book may know that they do not control Allah grace in any wise and that in Allah’s hand is all grace, which He grants to whomever He wishes and Allah is dispenser of a mighty grace. (29)

Circumstances of Revelation

Many commentators have narrated a circumstance of revelation for the above verses, the summary of which is as follows:

The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله sent Ja'far ibn Abi Talib with seventy people to the Negus (in Abyssinia). He visited the Negus and invited him to Islam; he accepted and believed. Upon returning from Abyssinia, forty people from that country who had believed said to Ja'far: "Permit us to come to the service of this Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and present our Islam to Him." They came to Medina with Ja'far. When they observed the financial poverty of the Muslims, they said to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله: "We have abundant wealth in our land; if You permit us, we will return to our country, bring our wealth back, and divide it with the Muslims." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله gave permission. They went, brought their wealth, and distributed it among themselves and the Muslims. At this moment, the verse: "الَّذِينَ آتَيْنَاهُمُ الْكِتَابَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ هُمْ بِهِ يُؤْمِنُونَ..." (Al-Qasas 52 to 54) was revealed, praising them.

When some individuals from the People of the Book who had not believed heard this phrase at the end of the aforementioned verses: "أُولَئِكَ يُؤْتَوْنَ أَجْرَهُمْ مَرَّتَيْنِ بِمَا صَبَرُوا" (Those will be given their reward twice for what they patiently endured), they stood against the Muslims and said: "O Muslims! Those who believe in your Book and our Book have two rewards; therefore, one who believes only in our Book has one reward, just like your reward! Thus, by your own admission, you have no virtue over us!" It was here that the above verses (O you who have believed, fear Allah...) were revealed, declaring that the Muslims also have two rewards, in addition to divine light and forgiveness. It then added: "Let the People of the Book know that they have no power to obtain anything from the grace and mercy of Allah!" [1]

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 244. The same meaning is narrated in the Tafsir of Abu al-Futuh Razi and Ruh al-Ma'ani under the verses in question with some variations.

Commentary

Those Who Have Two Shares of Divine Mercy

Since the previous verses discussed the People of the Book and the Christians, the verses under discussion serve as a completion to what preceded them:

First, He says: "O you who have believed, fear Allah and believe in His Messenger" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَآمِنُوا بِرَسُولِهِ).

Regarding who is addressed in this verse, there are two opinions among the commentators:

First, that the addressees are the believers; however, they are told that outward faith is not enough, but rather they must have a deep faith coupled with piety in action to obtain the multiple rewards mentioned at the end of the verse.

Second, that the addressees are the believers from the People of the Book; meaning, O you who have believed in the previous Prophets and Books, believe in the Prophet of Islam and fear Allah so that you may be included in His various rewards.

What could serve as evidence for the second interpretation is the "double reward" mentioned at the end of the verse—one reward for believing in the previous Prophets and one reward for believing in the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله.

However, this interpretation, besides being inconsistent with the following verse—as we will explain—is also not in harmony with the circumstance of revelation, nor with the general address of "O you who have believed" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا).

Therefore, it must be accepted that the addressees are all believers who had outwardly accepted the call of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله but had not yet attained a firm faith—a faith that illuminates the depths of their souls and manifests in their actions.

Then, in the continuation of the verse, He refers to three great blessings that result from deep faith and piety, saying: "If you do so, He will give you a double portion of His mercy and make for you a light by which you walk, and He will forgive you; and Allah is Forgiving and Merciful" (يُؤْتِكُمْ كِفْلَيْنِ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ وَيَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ نُوراً تَمْشُونَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ وَاللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

"Kifl" (rhyming with tifl) means a portion that suffices a person's need. A guarantor is called "kafil" because he suffices the problem of the other party and gives him his due portion. [1] In any case, the meaning of these two portions is the same as what appears in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 201: "Our Lord, give us in this world [that which is] good and in the Hereafter [that which is] good."

The possibility has also been raised that these two portions are: one for believing in the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the other for believing in the previous Prophets, as every Muslim is duty-bound to believe in all past Prophets and their Heavenly Books and to respect them all.

[1]: Some believe this word is derived from "kifl" (rhyming with dakal), which is something placed on the croup (the hindquarters) of animals so that the rider does not fall; hence, anything that serves as a safeguard is called "kifl," and if a guarantor is called "kafil," it is for this reason (Abu al-Futuh Razi under the verses in question). However, it is understood from Raghib that this word has two meanings: one desirable meaning, which is the first one, and another meaning of something lowly and worthless, likened to the croup of animals because whoever sits there fears falling (take note).

Some have also said that it refers to successive, doubled, and continuous rewards; combining these meanings is also possible.
