Regarding their second reward (وَ يَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ نُوراً تَمْشُونَ بِهِ), some commentators have said it refers to the light of faith that moves before the believers and on their right side on the Day of Resurrection, with which they pierce the darkness of the gathering place and advance toward Paradise and eternal bliss, as stated in verse 12 of this same Surah: "On the Day you see the believing men and believing women, their light proceeding before them and on their right."

While others consider it a reference to the light of the Quran that comes to the believers in this world, as we read in Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 15: "There has come to you from Allah a light and a clear Book."

However, it appears that the verse has an absolute and broad concept, restricted neither to this world nor the hereafter. In other words, faith and piety cause the veils to be lifted from the believer's heart, allowing him to see the face of realities without a shroud. In the light of that, a specific insight is granted to him which those without faith are deprived of. The fact that it is mentioned in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام that "light" in the above verse refers to the Infallible Imam whom the people follow is, in fact, an expression of one of its clear applications. [1] Finally, the third reward for the pious believers is the forgiveness of sins, for without it, no blessing is pleasant for man. First, he must be safe from divine punishment, then find his way through the light of faith and piety, and ultimately attain the doubled divine mercy.

[1]: These narrations are cited in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, pp. 252 and 253.

The next verse, which is the final verse of this Surah, states the reason for what was mentioned in the previous verse, saying: "These doubled divine rewards, in addition to the illumination and forgiveness, are so that the People of the Book may know that they are not able [to obtain] anything from the grace of Allah and that [all] grace is in His hand; He gives it to whom He wills, and Allah is the possessor of great grace" (لِئَلَّا يَعْلَمَ أَهْلُ الْكِتَابِ أَلَّا يَقْدِرُونَ عَلَى شَيْءٍ مِنْ فَضْلِ اللَّهِ وَأَنَّ الْفَضْلَ بِيَدِ اللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَنْ يَشَاءُ وَاللَّهُ ذُو الْفَضْلِ الْعَظِيمِ). [1] This is a response to those who said: "Allah gives two rewards to a group of the People of the Book who believed in Muhammad (according to the Muslims' belief); therefore, we who have not believed in Him have one reward, just like other Muslims!" The Quran answers them that Muslims generally have two rewards because they all believe in the Messenger of Allah and all previous Prophets. As for the group of the People of the Book who did not believe, they have no share, so they may know that divine mercy is not at their disposal to give to whom they wish and withhold from whom they wish! This verse can also be a response to the grandiosity and baseless claims of a group of Jews and Christians who considered Paradise and divine mercy exclusive to themselves and thought others were deprived of it: "And they say, 'None will enter Paradise except one who is a Jew or a Christian.' That is [merely] their wishful thinking. Say, 'Produce your proof, if you should be truthful.'"

[1]: There is a discussion among commentators whether the "la" in "li-alla ya'lama" is redundant or original. Many consider "la" to be redundant and for emphasis (as we mentioned above). Based on "la" being original, various meanings have been mentioned for the verse, including: the purpose is that the People of the Book should know that they too, if they accept faith and Islam, can provide divine grace for themselves (in other words, here a double negative becomes an affirmative); or that We gave all these blessings to the Muslims so that the People of the Book would not imagine that the Muslims have no share of divine grace. However, considering the end of the verse which says "and that grace is in the hand of Allah" and also the circumstance of revelation mentioned above, the redundancy of "la" seems more appropriate. In fact, in the opinion of some, in all cases where the sentence contains a negative meaning, "la" will be redundant, such as: "What prevented you that you did not prostrate (alla tasjuda) when I commanded you?" (Al-A'raf 12) and "And what will make you perceive that even if it came, they would not believe (la yu'minun)?" (Al-An'am 109) (Take note).

Key Point: The Relationship Between Piety and Insight

The Holy Quran has stated many effects for piety, among which is the removal of veils from human thought and heart.

The relationship between "faith and piety" and "insight" is mentioned in various verses of the Quran. For instance, in Surah Al-Anfal, verse 29, we read: "O you who have believed, if you fear Allah, He will grant you a criterion (furqan) [to judge between right and wrong]."

And in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 282, it says: "And fear Allah, and Allah teaches you." In the verses under discussion, this meaning was explicitly stated: "If you believe and fear Allah, He will provide for you a light by which you can walk!" The relationship between these two, in addition to spiritual aspects that are partly unknown to us, is also understandable through rational analysis. This is because the greatest obstacle to knowledge and the most significant veil over the human heart are rebellious whims and desires, long-term aspirations, and being enslaved by matter and the glitz and glamour of the world. These do not allow a person to judge correctly or see the face of realities as they are. When these dust clouds settle in the light of faith and piety, and these dark and gloomy clouds move away from the sky of the human soul, the sun of truth shines upon the tablet of the heart, and one perceives realities as they are. An indescribable pleasure from this correct and deep understanding becomes the portion of the believer, and he opens his way toward the sacred goals he possesses and moves forward.

Yes, it is piety that grants awareness to man, just as awareness grants piety to man—meaning these two have a mutual influence on each other. Thus, we read in a famous Hadith: "Were it not that the devils hover over the hearts of the children of Adam, they would surely look upon the kingdom (malakut) of the heavens."

To better understand this statement, we listen to the words of Ali عليه السلام who said: "There is no religion with passion—there is no intellect with passion—whoever follows his passion, it makes him blind and deaf, and humiliates him and leads him astray." [1]

O Lord! Protect us from the whims of the soul and grant us the success of piety and insight.

O Allah! All grace and mercies are in Your hand; do not deprive us of this Your ungrudging grace.

O God! Grant us the success of establishing truth, justice, and equity, and guarding the sanctity of the Book and the Balance in the light of utilizing "clear proofs" and standing against oppressors. Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah Al-Hadid. 20th of Rajab al-Murajjab, 1406 AH.
